title: Bronx

infobox.value: [Borough of New York City](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City")
[Vanessa Gibson](/wiki/Vanessa_Gibson "Vanessa Gibson") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")) – *(Borough of the Bronx)*

([/brɒŋks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BRONKS*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is the northernmost of the five [boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), coextensive with **Bronx County**, in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). The borough shares a land border with [Westchester County, New York](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") to its north; to its south and west, the New York City borough of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") lies across the [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River"); and to its south and east is the borough of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), across the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"). The Bronx, the only New York City borough located primarily on the [U.S. mainland](/wiki/U.S._mainland "U.S. mainland"), has a land area of 42 square miles (109 km2) and a population of 1,472,654 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[2]](#cite_note-2020CensusMap-2) It has the fourth-largest area, fourth-highest population, and third-highest [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") of the boroughs.[[5]](#cite_note-density-5)

**The Bronx** ([/brɒŋks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BRONKS*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is the northernmost of the five [boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), coextensive with **Bronx County**, in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). The borough shares a land border with [Westchester County, New York](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") to its north; to its south and west, the New York City borough of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") lies across the [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River"); and to its south and east is the borough of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), across the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"). The Bronx, the only New York City borough located primarily on the [U.S. mainland](/wiki/U.S._mainland "U.S. mainland"), has a land area of 42 square miles (109 km2) and a population of 1,472,654 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[2]](#cite_note-2020CensusMap-2) It has the fourth-largest area, fourth-highest population, and third-highest [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") of the boroughs.[[5]](#cite_note-density-5)

The Bronx is divided by the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") into a hillier section in the [west](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx"), and a flatter [eastern](/wiki/East_Bronx "East Bronx") section. East and west street names are divided by [Jerome Avenue](/wiki/Jerome_Avenue "Jerome Avenue"). The West Bronx was annexed to New York City in 1874, and the areas east of the Bronx River in 1895.[[6]](#cite_note-ultan-6) Bronx County was separated from New York County (modern-day Manhattan) in 1914.[[7]](#cite_note-courtstart-7) About a quarter of the Bronx's area is open space,[[8]](#cite_note-blooming-8) including [Woodlawn Cemetery](/wiki/Woodlawn_Cemetery_(Bronx,_New_York) "Woodlawn Cemetery (Bronx, New York)"), [Van Cortlandt Park](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park"), [Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park"), the [New York Botanical Garden](/wiki/New_York_Botanical_Garden "New York Botanical Garden"), and the [Bronx Zoo](/wiki/Bronx_Zoo "Bronx Zoo") in the borough's north and center. The [Thain Family Forest](/wiki/Thain_Family_Forest "Thain Family Forest") at the New York Botanical Garden is thousands of years old and is New York City's largest remaining tract of the original forest that once covered the city.[[9]](#cite_note-9) These open spaces are primarily on land reserved in the late 19th century as urban development progressed north and east from Manhattan. The Bronx is also home to [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball").

The word "Bronx" originated with [Jonas Bronck](/wiki/Jonas_Bronck "Jonas Bronck"), who established the first European settlement in the area as part of the [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") colony in 1639.[[10]](#cite_note-Faroeislands-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Van_Rensselaer_1909_161-12) European settlers displaced the native [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") after 1643. In the 19th and 20th centuries, the Bronx received many immigrant and migrant groups as it was transformed into an urban community, first from European countries (especially [Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), [Germany](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), [Italy](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans"), and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe")) and later from the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") region (particularly [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans_in_the_United_States "Puerto Ricans in the United States"), [Trinidad](/wiki/Trinidadian_Americans "Trinidadian Americans"), [Haiti](/wiki/Haitian_Americans "Haitian Americans"), [Guyana](/wiki/Guyanese_Americans "Guyanese Americans"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaican_Americans "Jamaican Americans"), [Barbados](/wiki/Barbadian_Americans "Barbadian Americans"), and the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans")) and [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") (mainly [Ghana](/wiki/Ghanaian_Americans "Ghanaian Americans") and [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigerian_Americans "Nigerian Americans")). [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") migrants from the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") as well as [Panamanians](/wiki/Panamanian_Americans "Panamanian Americans"), [Hondurans](/wiki/Honduran_Americans "Honduran Americans"), and [South Asians](/wiki/South_Asian_ethnic_groups "South Asian ethnic groups") also established themselves in the borough in large numbers.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The Bronx contains what had been the poorest [of all 435 U.S. congressional districts](/wiki/Congressional_districts_of_the_United_States "Congressional districts of the United States"), [New York's 15th](/wiki/New_York%27s_15th_congressional_district "New York's 15th congressional district"), until redistricting following the 2020 census.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The borough also features upper- and middle-income neighborhoods, such as [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx"), [Fieldston](/wiki/Fieldston,_Bronx "Fieldston, Bronx"), [Spuyten Duyvil](/wiki/Spuyten_Duyvil,_Bronx "Spuyten Duyvil, Bronx"), [Schuylerville](/wiki/Schuylerville,_Bronx "Schuylerville, Bronx"), [Pelham Bay](/wiki/Pelham_Bay,_Bronx "Pelham Bay, Bronx"), [Pelham Gardens](/wiki/Pelham_Gardens,_Bronx "Pelham Gardens, Bronx"), [Morris Park](/wiki/Morris_Park,_Bronx "Morris Park, Bronx"), and [Country Club](/wiki/Country_Club,_Bronx "Country Club, Bronx").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Barone-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Stat_Abst-17) Parts of the Bronx saw a steep decline in population, livable housing and quality of life starting from the mid-to-late 1960s and lasting well into the 1980s, including a [wave of arson in the late 1970s](/wiki/1970s_South_Bronx_building_fires "1970s South Bronx building fires").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx"), in particular, experienced severe [urban decay](/wiki/Urban_decay "Urban decay"). The borough began experiencing new population growth starting in the late 1990s and continuing to the present day.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Etymology and naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and naming")]

### Early names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early names")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Bronx1867.jpg/330px-Bronx1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronx1867.jpg)

A map of southern Westchester County in 1867. This, along with the southern part of the former Town of [Yonkers](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York"), became the Bronx.

The Bronx was called **Rananchqua**[[20]](#cite_note-NYPL-20) by the native [Siwanoy](/wiki/Siwanoy "Siwanoy")[[21]](#cite_note-21) band of [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), also known historically as *the Delawares*. Other [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") knew the Bronx as *Keskeskeck*.[[22]](#cite_note-ellis-p55-22) It was divided by the Aquahung River (now known in English as the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River")).

The "Bronx" name derives from [Jonas Bronck](/wiki/Jonas_Bronck "Jonas Bronck") (c. 1600–1643), a European settler whose precise origins are disputed. Documents indicate he was a Swedish-born immigrant from [Komstad, Norra Ljunga parish](/wiki/Komstad "Komstad") in [Småland](/wiki/Sm%C3%A5land "Småland"), Sweden, who arrived in [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") during the spring of 1639.[[12]](#cite_note-Van_Rensselaer_1909_161-12)[[23]](#cite_note-Hansen_1950-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Burrows-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Bronck became the first recorded European settler in the present-day Bronx and built a farm named "Emmaus" close to what today is the corner of Willis Avenue and 132nd Street in [Mott Haven](/wiki/Mott_Haven,_Bronx "Mott Haven, Bronx").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

He leased land from the [Dutch West India Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") on the neck of the mainland immediately north of the Dutch settlement of [New Haarlem](/wiki/History_of_Harlem#1637–1866 "History of Harlem") (on [Manhattan Island](/wiki/Manhattan_Island "Manhattan Island")), and bought additional tracts from the local tribes. He eventually accumulated 500 acres (200 ha) between the [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River") and the Aquahung, which became known as *Bronck's River* or *the [Bronx](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") [River]*. Dutch and English settlers referred to the area as *Bronck's Land*.[[23]](#cite_note-Hansen_1950-23)

The American poet [William Bronk](/wiki/William_Bronk "William Bronk") was a descendant of Pieter Bronck, probably Jonas Bronck's nephew or cousin, as there was an age difference of 16 years.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Much work on the Swedish claim has been undertaken by Brian G. Andersson, former Commissioner of New York City's Department of Records, who helped organize a 375th Anniversary celebration in Bronck's hometown in 2014.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Use of definite article

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Use of definite article")]

The Bronx is referred to with the [definite article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") as "the Bronx" or "The Bronx", both legally and colloquially.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The "County of the Bronx" also takes "the" immediately before "Bronx" in formal references, like the coextensive "Borough of the Bronx". The [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") uses "Bronx, NY" for mailing addresses.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The definite article comes from the Bronx River and first appeared in the "Annexed District of The Bronx", created in 1874 out of part of [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York").[[34]](#cite_note-ny1.com-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Steven_Hess_1987-35)

It was continued in the "Borough of The Bronx", created in 1898, which included a larger annexation from Westchester County in 1895.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The use of the definite article is attributed to the style of referring to rivers.[[34]](#cite_note-ny1.com-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Steven_Hess_1987-35) A time-worn story purportedly explaining the use of the definite article in the borough's name says it stems from the phrase "visiting the Broncks", referring to the settler's family.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The capitalization of the borough's name is sometimes disputed. Generally, the definite article is lowercase in place names ("the Bronx") except in some official references. The definite article is capitalized ("The Bronx") at the beginning of a sentence or in any other situation when a normally lowercase word would be capitalized.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Some people and groups refer to the borough with a capital letter at all times, such as Bronx Borough Historian [Lloyd Ultan](/wiki/Lloyd_Ultan_(historian) "Lloyd Ultan (historian)"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) [The Bronx County Historical Society](/wiki/The_Bronx_County_Historical_Society "The Bronx County Historical Society"), and the Bronx-based organization Great and Glorious Grand Army of The Bronx, arguing the definite article is part of the proper name.[[40]](#cite_note-WhyBronx-40)[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41) In particular, the Great and Glorious Grand Army of The Bronx is leading efforts to make the city refer to the borough with an uppercase definite article in all uses, comparing the lowercase article in the Bronx's name to "not capitalizing the 's' in 'Staten Island'".[[41]](#cite_note-:0-41)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of the Bronx](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Bronx "Timeline of the Bronx").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/History_of_Bronx_borough%2C_city_of_New_York%3B_%28IA_historyofbronxbo00comf%29.pdf/page9-250px-History_of_Bronx_borough%2C_city_of_New_York%3B_%28IA_historyofbronxbo00comf%29.pdf.jpg)](/w/index.php?title=File:History_of_Bronx_borough,_city_of_New_York;_(IA_historyofbronxbo00comf).pdf&page=9)

The first published book of Bronx history: *History of Bronx Borough, City of New York* by Randall Comfort

Originally, the area was part of the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape")'s [Lenapehoking](/wiki/Lenapehoking "Lenapehoking") territory inhabited by [Siwanoy](/wiki/Siwanoy "Siwanoy") of the [Wappinger](/wiki/Wappinger "Wappinger") Confederacy.[[42]](#cite_note-42) European colonization of the Bronx began in 1639.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Over time, European colonists converted the borough into farmlands. The Bronx was originally part of [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York"), but it was ceded to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") in two major parts (the [West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx") in 1874 and the [East Bronx](/wiki/East_Bronx "East Bronx") in 1895), subsequently becoming Bronx County.

### Before 1914

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Before 1914")]

See also: [List of former municipalities in New York City](/wiki/List_of_former_municipalities_in_New_York_City "List of former municipalities in New York City")

The Bronx's development is directly connected to its strategic location between [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and New York ([Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")). Control over the bridges across the Harlem River plagued the period of British colonial rule. The King's Bridge, built in 1693 where [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") reached the [Spuyten Duyvil Creek](/wiki/Spuyten_Duyvil_Creek "Spuyten Duyvil Creek"), was a possession of [Frederick Philipse](/wiki/Frederick_Philipse "Frederick Philipse"), lord of [Philipse Manor](/wiki/Philipse_Manor_Hall_State_Historic_Site "Philipse Manor Hall State Historic Site").[[44]](#cite_note-CrotonHarlemFSEIS-7-12-44) Local farmers on both sides of the creek resented the tolls, and in 1759, Jacobus Dyckman and Benjamin Palmer led them in building a free bridge across the Harlem River.[[45]](#cite_note-Fordam-45) After the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), the King's Bridge toll was abolished.[[44]](#cite_note-CrotonHarlemFSEIS-7-12-44)[[46]](#cite_note-Jenkins1912-46)

The territory now contained within Bronx County was originally part of [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York"), one of the 12 original counties of the English [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York"). The present Bronx County was contained in the town of Westchester and parts of the towns in [Yonkers](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York"), [Eastchester](/wiki/Eastchester,_New_York "Eastchester, New York"), and [Pelham](/wiki/Pelham,_New_York "Pelham, New York"). In 1846, a new town was created by division of Westchester, called West Farms. The town of [Morrisania](/wiki/Morrisania,_Bronx "Morrisania, Bronx") was created, in turn, from West Farms in 1855. In 1873, the town of [Kingsbridge](/wiki/Kingsbridge,_Bronx "Kingsbridge, Bronx") was established within the former borders of the town of Yonkers, roughly corresponding to the modern Bronx neighborhoods of Kingsbridge, [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx"), and [Woodlawn Heights](/wiki/Woodlawn_Heights,_Bronx "Woodlawn Heights, Bronx"), and included [Woodlawn Cemetery](/wiki/Woodlawn_Cemetery_(Bronx,_New_York) "Woodlawn Cemetery (Bronx, New York)").

Among the famous people who settled in the Bronx during the 19th and early 20th centuries were author [Willa Cather](/wiki/Willa_Cather "Willa Cather"), tobacco merchant [Pierre Lorillard](/wiki/Pierre_Lorillard_IV "Pierre Lorillard IV"), and inventor [Jordan L. Mott](/wiki/Jordan_L._Mott "Jordan L. Mott"), who established [Mott Haven](/wiki/Mott_Haven "Mott Haven") to house the workers at his iron works.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The consolidation of the Bronx into New York City proceeded in two stages. In 1873, the state legislature annexed Kingsbridge, West Farms, and Morrisania to New York, effective in 1874; the three towns were soon abolished in the process.[[48]](#cite_note-thorne-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In 1895, the whole territory east of the Bronx River was annexed to the city three years before New York's consolidation with [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"). This included the Town of Westchester, which had voted against consolidation in 1894, and parts of Eastchester and Pelham.[[6]](#cite_note-ultan-6)[[48]](#cite_note-thorne-48)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-NYT1973-52) The maritime community of [City Island](/wiki/City_Island,_Bronx "City Island, Bronx") voted to join the city in 1896.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Following these two annexations, the Bronx's territory had moved from Westchester County into New York County, which already included Manhattan and the rest of pre-1874 New York City.

On January 1, 1898, the consolidated [City of New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York") was born, including the Bronx as one of the five distinct [boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)"). It remained part of New York County until Bronx County was created in 1914.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

On April 19, 1912, those parts of New York County which had been annexed from Westchester County in previous decades were newly constituted as Bronx County, the 62nd and last county to be created by the state, effective in 1914.[[48]](#cite_note-thorne-48)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Bronx County's courts opened for business on January 2, 1914, the same day that [John P. Mitchel](/wiki/John_P._Mitchel "John P. Mitchel") started work as [Mayor of New York City](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City").[[7]](#cite_note-courtstart-7) [Marble Hill, Manhattan](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan"), was now connected to the Bronx by filling in the former waterway, but it is not part of the borough or county.[[56]](#cite_note-NYT1993-56)

### After 1914

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: After 1914")]

The history of the Bronx during the 20th century may be divided into four periods: a boom period during 1900–1929, with a population growth by a factor of six from 200,000 in 1900 to 1.3 million in 1930. The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and post World War II years saw a slowing of growth leading into an eventual decline. The mid to late century were hard times, as the Bronx changed during 1950–1985 from a predominantly moderate-income to a predominantly lower-income area with high rates of violent crime and poverty in some areas. The Bronx has experienced an economic and developmental resurgence starting in the late 1980s that continues into today.[[57]](#cite_note-Olmsted_1989;_Olmsted_1998-57)

#### New York City expands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: New York City expands")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Bronx_1900.jpg/330px-Bronx_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronx_1900.jpg)

The [Grand Concourse](/wiki/Grand_Concourse_(Bronx) "Grand Concourse (Bronx)") and [161st Street](/wiki/161st_Street_(Bronx) "161st Street (Bronx)") as they appeared around 1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Simpson_Street_Station.jpg/330px-Simpson_Street_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Simpson_Street_Station.jpg)

The [Simpson Street](/wiki/Simpson_Street_(IRT_White_Plains_Road_Line) "Simpson Street (IRT White Plains Road Line)") elevated station was built in 1904. It was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 2004.

The Bronx was a mostly rural area for many generations, with small farms supplying the city markets. In the late 19th century, it grew into a railroad suburb. Faster transportation enabled rapid population growth in the late 19th century, involving the move from horse-drawn street cars to elevated railways and the subway system, which linked to Manhattan in 1904.[[57]](#cite_note-Olmsted_1989;_Olmsted_1998-57)

The South Bronx was a manufacturing center for many years, and was noted as a center of [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") manufacturing in the early part of the 20th century. In 1919, the Bronx was the site of 63 piano factories employing more than 5,000 workers.[[58]](#cite_note-Piano_Workers_May_Strike-58)

Before the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the Bronx hosted the rather small [1918 World's Fair](/wiki/Bronx_International_Exposition_of_Science,_Arts_and_Industries "Bronx International Exposition of Science, Arts and Industries") at 177th Street and DeVoe Avenue.[[6]](#cite_note-ultan-6)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The Bronx underwent rapid urban growth after World War I. Extensions of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") contributed to the increase in population as thousands of immigrants came to the Bronx, resulting in a major boom in residential construction.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Among these groups, many [Irish Americans](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans"), and especially [Jewish Americans](/wiki/Jewish_Americans "Jewish Americans") settled here. [French](/wiki/French_American "French American"), [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American"), and other immigrants moved into the borough. As evidence of the change in population, by 1937, 592,185 Jews lived in the Bronx (43.9% of the borough's population),[[61]](#cite_note-61) while only 54,000 Jews lived in the borough in 2011. Many [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") still stand in the Bronx, but most have been converted to other uses.[[62]](#cite_note-Remembrance-62)

#### Change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Change")]

[Bootleggers](/wiki/Rum-running "Rum-running") and gangs were active in the Bronx during [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"), between 1920 and 1933. Irish, Italian, Jewish, and Polish gangs smuggled in most of the illegal whiskey, and the oldest sections of the borough became poverty-stricken.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Police Commissioner Richard Enright said that [speakeasies](/wiki/Speakeasy "Speakeasy") provided a place for "the vicious elements, bootleggers, gamblers and their friends in all walks of life" to cooperate and to "evade the law, escape punishment for their crimes, [and] to deter the police from doing their duty".[[64]](#cite_note-64)

In 1945, the [Bronx Marian apparitions](/wiki/Bronx_Marian_apparitions "Bronx Marian apparitions") drew 30,000 pilgrims to the area to see the site of an alleged vision of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus").[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Between 1930 and 1960, moderate and upper income Bronxites, predominantly non-Hispanic Whites, [began to relocate](/wiki/White_flight "White flight") from the borough's southwestern neighborhoods. This migration has left a mostly poor African American and Hispanic, largely [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans_in_the_United_States "Puerto Ricans in the United States"), population in the West Bronx. A significant factor that shifted the racial and economic demographics was the construction of [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City,_Bronx "Co-op City, Bronx"), built to house middle-class residents in family-sized apartments. The high-rise complex played a significant role in draining middle-class residents from older tenement buildings in the borough's southern and western fringes. Most predominantly non-Hispanic White communities today [are in the eastern and northwestern sections of the borough](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Bronx "Demographics of the Bronx").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Secretary_of_H.U.D._Patricia_Harris%2C_Jimmy_Carter_and_New_York_Mayor_Abraham_Beame_tour_the_South_Bronx._-_NARA_-_176392.jpg/330px-Secretary_of_H.U.D._Patricia_Harris%2C_Jimmy_Carter_and_New_York_Mayor_Abraham_Beame_tour_the_South_Bronx._-_NARA_-_176392.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_of_H.U.D._Patricia_Harris,_Jimmy_Carter_and_New_York_Mayor_Abraham_Beame_tour_the_South_Bronx._-_NARA_-_176392.jpg)

President [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") and New York Mayor [Abraham Beame](/wiki/Abraham_Beame "Abraham Beame") tour the [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx") in 1977

From the mid-1960s to the early 1980s, the [quality of life](/wiki/Quality_of_life "Quality of life") changed for some Bronx residents. Historians and social scientists have suggested many factors, including the theory that [Robert Moses](/wiki/Robert_Moses "Robert Moses")' [Cross Bronx Expressway](/wiki/Cross_Bronx_Expressway "Cross Bronx Expressway") destroyed existing residential neighborhoods and created instant slums, as put forward in [Robert Caro](/wiki/Robert_Caro "Robert Caro")'s biography *[The Power Broker](/wiki/The_Power_Broker "The Power Broker")*.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Another factor in the Bronx's decline may have been the development of [high-rise](/wiki/High-rise "High-rise") [housing projects](/wiki/Public_housing#United_States "Public housing"), particularly in the [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx").[[68]](#cite_note-68) Yet another factor may have been a reduction in the real estate listings and property-related financial services offered in some areas of the Bronx, such as [mortgage loans](/wiki/Mortgage_loan "Mortgage loan") or insurance policies—a process known as [redlining](/wiki/Redlining "Redlining"). Others have suggested a "[planned shrinkage](/wiki/Planned_shrinkage "Planned shrinkage")" of municipal services, such as fire-fighting.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) There was also much debate as to whether [rent control](/wiki/Rent_control "Rent control") laws had made it less profitable (or more costly) for landlords to maintain existing buildings with their existing tenants than to abandon or destroy those buildings.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 1970s, parts of the Bronx were plagued by a wave of arson. The burning of buildings was predominantly in the poorest communities, such as the South Bronx. One explanation of this event was that landlords decided to burn their low property-value buildings and take the insurance money, as it was easier for them to get insurance money than to try to refurbish a dilapidated building or sell a building in a severely distressed area.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The Bronx became identified with a high rate of poverty and unemployment, which was mainly a persistent problem in the South Bronx.[[73]](#cite_note-Gonzalez_2004-73) There were cases where tenants set fire to the building they lived in so they could qualify for emergency relocations by city social service agencies to better residences, sometimes being relocated to other parts of the city.

Out of 289 census tracts in the Bronx borough, 7 tracts lost more than 97% of their buildings to arson and abandonment between 1970 and 1980; another 44 tracts had more than 50% of their buildings meet the same fate. By the early 1980s, the Bronx was considered the most blighted urban area in the country, particularly the South Bronx which experienced a loss of 60% of the population and 40% of housing units. However, starting in the 1990s, many of the burned-out and run-down tenements were replaced by new housing units.[[73]](#cite_note-Gonzalez_2004-73)

In May 1984, [New York Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") justice [Peter J. McQuillan](/wiki/Peter_J._McQuillan "Peter J. McQuillan") ruled that [Marble Hill, Manhattan](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan"), was simultaneously part of the Borough of Manhattan (not the Borough of the Bronx) and part of Bronx County (not New York County)[[74]](#cite_note-74) and the matter was definitively settled later that year when the [New York Legislature](/wiki/New_York_Legislature "New York Legislature") overwhelmingly passed legislation declaring the neighborhood part of both New York County and the Borough of Manhattan and made this clarification retroactive to 1938, as reflected on the official maps of the city.[[75]](#cite_note-Bloom_1995-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### Revitalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Revitalization")]

[![four-story houses along a city street](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Melrosebx1.JPG/250px-Melrosebx1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Melrosebx1.JPG)

[Row houses](/wiki/Row_houses "Row houses") on a location where there was once burnt rubble. The Bronx has since seen revitalization.

Since the late 1980s, significant development has occurred in the Bronx, first stimulated by the city's "Ten-Year Housing Plan"[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) and community members working to rebuild the social, economic and environmental infrastructure by creating [affordable housing](/wiki/Affordable_housing "Affordable housing"). Groups affiliated with churches in the South Bronx erected the Nehemiah Homes with about 1,000 units. The grass roots organization Nos Quedamos' endeavor known as Melrose Commons[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) began to rebuild areas in the South Bronx.[[83]](#cite_note-83) The [IRT White Plains Road Line](/wiki/IRT_White_Plains_Road_Line "IRT White Plains Road Line") ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)") and ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)") trains) began to show an increase in riders. Chains such as [Marshalls](/wiki/Marshalls "Marshalls"), [Staples](/wiki/Staples_Inc. "Staples Inc."), and [Target](/wiki/Target_Corporation "Target Corporation") opened stores in the Bronx. More bank branches opened in the Bronx as a whole (rising from 106 in 1997 to 149 in 2007), although not primarily in poor or minority neighborhoods, while the Bronx still has fewer branches per person than other boroughs.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[87]](#cite_note-87)

[![The Bronx – All-America City sign](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/The_Bronx_-_All-America_City_sign_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-The_Bronx_-_All-America_City_sign_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Bronx_-_All-America_City_sign_-_panoramio.jpg)

The Bronx – All-America City sign

In 1997, the Bronx was designated an *[All America City](/wiki/All_America_City "All America City")* by the [National Civic League](/wiki/National_Civic_League "National Civic League"), acknowledging its comeback from the decline of the mid-century.[[88]](#cite_note-88) In 2006, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that "construction cranes have become the borough's new visual metaphor, replacing the window decals of the 1980s in which pictures of potted plants and drawn curtains were placed in the windows of abandoned buildings."[[89]](#cite_note-thonx-89) The borough has experienced substantial new building construction since 2002. Between 2002 and June 2007, 33,687 new units of housing were built or were under way and $4.8 billion has been invested in new housing. In the first six months of 2007 alone total investment in new residential development was $965 million and 5,187 residential units were scheduled to be completed. Much of the new development is springing up in formerly vacant lots across the South Bronx.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In addition there came a revitalization of the existing housing market in areas such as Hunts Point, the Lower Concourse, and the neighborhoods surrounding the Third Avenue Bridge as people buy apartments and renovate them.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Several boutique and chain hotels opened in the 2010s in the [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx").[[92]](#cite_note-92)

New developments are underway. The [Bronx General Post Office](/wiki/Bronx_General_Post_Office "Bronx General Post Office")[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) on the corner of the Grand Concourse and East 149th Street is being converted into a market place, boutiques, restaurants and office space with a USPS concession.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The [Kingsbridge Armory](/wiki/Kingsbridge_Armory "Kingsbridge Armory"), often cited as the largest armory in the world, is currently slated for redevelopment.
Under consideration for future development is the construction of a platform over the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway")'s [Concourse Yard](/wiki/Concourse_Yard "Concourse Yard") adjacent to [Lehman College](/wiki/Lehman_College "Lehman College"). The construction permitted approximately 2,000,000 square feet (190,000 m2) of development and cost US$350–500 million.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Despite significant investment compared to the post war period, many exacerbated social problems remain including high rates of violent crime, substance abuse, overcrowding, and substandard housing conditions.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100) The Bronx has the highest rate of poverty in New York City, and the greater South Bronx is the poorest area.[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of New York City](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York_City "Geography of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/New_York_City_location_Bronx.svg/250px-New_York_City_location_Bronx.svg.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_location_Bronx.svg)

The location of the Bronx (red) within New York City

### Location and physical features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Location and physical features")]

See also: [List of smaller islands in New York City](/wiki/List_of_smaller_islands_in_New_York_City "List of smaller islands in New York City")

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau"), Bronx County has a total area of 57 square miles (150 km2), of which 42 square miles (110 km2) is land and 15 square miles (39 km2) (27%) is water.[[103]](#cite_note-GR1-103)

The Bronx is New York City's northernmost borough, New York State's southernmost mainland county and the only part of New York City that is almost entirely on the North American mainland, unlike the other four boroughs that are either islands or located on islands.[[104]](#cite_note-GR6-104) The [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") of the [West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx") is primarily *Fordham [gneiss](/wiki/Gneiss "Gneiss")*, a high-grade heavily banded [metamorphic rock](/wiki/Metamorphic_rock "Metamorphic rock") containing significant amounts of [pink feldspar](/wiki/Feldspar "Feldspar").[[105]](#cite_note-105) Marble Hill – politically part of Manhattan but now physically attached to the Bronx – is so-called because of the formation of [Inwood marble](/wiki/Inwood_marble "Inwood marble") there as well as in [Inwood, Manhattan](/wiki/Inwood,_Manhattan "Inwood, Manhattan"), and parts of the Bronx and Westchester County.

The [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") separates the Bronx on the west from [Alpine](/wiki/Alpine,_New_Jersey "Alpine, New Jersey"), [Tenafly](/wiki/Tenafly,_New_Jersey "Tenafly, New Jersey") and [Englewood Cliffs](/wiki/Englewood_Cliffs,_New_Jersey "Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey") in [Bergen County, New Jersey](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey"). The [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River") separates it from the island of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") to the southwest. The [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") separates it from [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") to the southeast. To the east, [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") separates it from [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York") in western Long Island. Directly north of the Bronx are (from west to east) the adjoining [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") communities of [Yonkers](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York"), [Mount Vernon](/wiki/Mount_Vernon,_New_York "Mount Vernon, New York"), [Pelham Manor](/wiki/Pelham_Manor,_New_York "Pelham Manor, New York") and [New Rochelle](/wiki/New_Rochelle,_New_York "New Rochelle, New York"). There is also a short southern land boundary with [Marble Hill](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan") in the Borough of Manhattan, over the filled-in former course of the [Spuyten Duyvil Creek](/wiki/Spuyten_Duyvil_Creek "Spuyten Duyvil Creek"); Marble Hill's postal [ZIP code](/wiki/ZIP_code "ZIP code"), telephonic [area codes](/wiki/Area_codes "Area codes") and fire service, however, are shared with the Bronx and not Manhattan.[[56]](#cite_note-NYT1993-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/New_York_aerial_night_2018e.jpg/250px-New_York_aerial_night_2018e.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_aerial_night_2018e.jpg)

An aerial view of the Bronx from the east at night

The [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") flows south from Westchester County through the borough, emptying into the East River; it is the only entirely [freshwater](/wiki/Freshwater "Freshwater") river in New York City.[[106]](#cite_note-nytimes-106) It separates the West Bronx from the schist of the [East Bronx](/wiki/East_Bronx "East Bronx"). A smaller river, the [Hutchinson River](/wiki/Hutchinson_River "Hutchinson River") (named after the religious leader [Anne Hutchinson](/wiki/Anne_Hutchinson "Anne Hutchinson"), killed along its banks in 1641), passes through the East Bronx and empties into [Eastchester Bay](/wiki/Eastchester_Bay "Eastchester Bay").

The Bronx includes several small islands in the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") and [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"), such as [City Island](/wiki/City_Island,_New_York "City Island, New York") and [Hart Island](/wiki/Hart_Island_(New_York) "Hart Island (New York)"). [Rikers Island](/wiki/Rikers_Island "Rikers Island") in the East River, home to the large jail complex for the entire city, is also part of the Bronx.

The Bronx's highest elevation at 280 feet (85 m) is in the northwest corner, west of [Van Cortlandt Park](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park") and in the Chapel Farm area near the [Riverdale Country School](/wiki/Riverdale_Country_School "Riverdale Country School").[[107]](#cite_note-107) The opposite (southeastern) side of the Bronx has four large low peninsulas or "necks" of low-lying land that jut into the waters of the East River and were once [salt marsh](/wiki/Salt_marsh "Salt marsh"): Hunt's Point, Clason's Point, Screvin's Neck and [Throggs Neck](/wiki/Throggs_Neck "Throggs Neck"). Further up the coastline, [Rodman's Neck](/wiki/Rodman%27s_Neck "Rodman's Neck") lies between [Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park") in the northeast and [City Island](/wiki/City_Island,_New_York "City Island, New York"). The Bronx's irregular shoreline extends for 75 square miles (194 km2).[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Parks and open space

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Parks and open space")]

See also: [Category:Parks in the Bronx](/wiki/Category:Parks_in_the_Bronx "Category:Parks in the Bronx")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Bronx_transit_and_landmarks_in_1896_%28NY_Times%29_-_re-tinted.png/500px-Bronx_transit_and_landmarks_in_1896_%28NY_Times%29_-_re-tinted.png)](/wiki/File:Bronx_transit_and_landmarks_in_1896_(NY_Times)_-_re-tinted.png)

[Marble Hill](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan") is in pink, cut off by water from the rest of Manhattan in orange. [Van Cortlandt](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park"), [Pelham Bay](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park") and [Crotona Parks](/wiki/Crotona_Park "Crotona Park") are light green, as is [Bronx Park](/wiki/Bronx_Park "Bronx Park") (now home to the [New York Botanical Garden](/wiki/New_York_Botanical_Garden "New York Botanical Garden") and [Bronx Zoo](/wiki/Bronx_Zoo "Bronx Zoo")). [Woodlawn Cemetery](/wiki/Woodlawn_Cemetery_(Bronx,_New_York) "Woodlawn Cemetery (Bronx, New York)") is medium green, sports facilities are dark green. The not-yet-built [Jerome Park Reservoir](/wiki/Jerome_Park_Reservoir "Jerome Park Reservoir") light blue, St. John's College (now [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University")) is violet, and the city limits of the newly expanded New York are red.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

Although Bronx County was the third most densely populated county in the United States in 2022 (after [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn")),[[112]](#cite_note-CensusDensity2022-112) 7,000 acres (28 km2) of the Bronx—about one fifth of the Bronx's area, and one quarter of its land area—is given over to parkland.[[8]](#cite_note-blooming-8)[[113]](#cite_note-113) The vision of a system of major Bronx parks connected by park-like thoroughfares is usually attributed to [John Mullaly](/wiki/John_Mullaly "John Mullaly").

[Woodlawn Cemetery](/wiki/Woodlawn_Cemetery_(Bronx,_New_York) "Woodlawn Cemetery (Bronx, New York)"), located on 400 acres (160 ha) and one of the largest cemeteries in New York City, sits on the western bank of the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") near [Yonkers](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York"). It opened in 1863, in what was then the town of Yonkers, at the time a rural area. Since the first burial in 1865, more than 300,000 people have been interred there.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

The borough's northern side includes the largest park in New York City—[Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park"), which includes [Orchard Beach](/wiki/Orchard_Beach,_New_York "Orchard Beach, New York")—and the third-largest, [Van Cortlandt Park](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park"), which is west of Woodlawn Cemetery and borders [Yonkers](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York").[[115]](#cite_note-Van_Cortlandt_Park_:_NYC_Parks-115) Also in the northern Bronx, [Wave Hill](/wiki/Wave_Hill_(New_York) "Wave Hill (New York)"), the former estate of [George W. Perkins](/wiki/George_Walbridge_Perkins "George Walbridge Perkins")—known for a historic house, gardens, changing site-specific art installations and concerts—overlooks the [New Jersey Palisades](/wiki/New_Jersey_Palisades "New Jersey Palisades") from a promontory on the [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") in [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx").[[115]](#cite_note-Van_Cortlandt_Park_:_NYC_Parks-115)

Nearer the borough's center, and along the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River"), is [Bronx Park](/wiki/Bronx_Park "Bronx Park"). Its northern end houses the [New York Botanical Gardens](/wiki/New_York_Botanical_Gardens "New York Botanical Gardens"), which preserve the last patch of the original [hemlock](/wiki/Tsuga "Tsuga") forest that once covered the county, and its southern end the [Bronx Zoo](/wiki/Bronx_Zoo "Bronx Zoo"), the largest urban zoological gardens in the United States.[[116]](#cite_note-fordzoo-116) In 1904, the Chestnut Blight pathogen (*[Cryphonectria parasitica](/wiki/Cryphonectria_parasitica "Cryphonectria parasitica")*) was found for the first time outside of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), at the Bronx Zoo.[[117]](#cite_note-VanDerPlank-117) Over the next 40 years it spread throughout [eastern North America](/wiki/Eastern_North_America "Eastern North America") and killed back essentially every American Chestnut (*[Castanea dentata](/wiki/Castanea_dentata "Castanea dentata")*), causing ecological and economic devastation.[[117]](#cite_note-VanDerPlank-117)

Just south of [Van Cortlandt Park](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park") is the [Jerome Park Reservoir](/wiki/Jerome_Park_Reservoir "Jerome Park Reservoir"), surrounded by 2 miles (3 km) of stone walls and bordering several small parks in the [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_Bronx "Bedford Park, Bronx") neighborhood; the [reservoir](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir") was built in the 1890s on the site of the former [Jerome Park Racetrack](/wiki/Jerome_Park_Racetrack "Jerome Park Racetrack").[[118]](#cite_note-118) Further south is [Crotona Park](/wiki/Crotona_Park "Crotona Park"), home to a 3.3-acre (1.3 ha) lake, 28 species of trees, and a large swimming pool.[[119]](#cite_note-119) The land for these parks, and many others, was bought by New York City in 1888, while land was still open and inexpensive, in anticipation of future needs and future pressures for development.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Some of the acquired land was set aside for the [Grand Concourse](/wiki/Grand_Concourse_(Bronx) "Grand Concourse (Bronx)") and [Pelham Parkway](/wiki/Pelham_Parkway "Pelham Parkway"), the first of a series of [boulevards](/wiki/Boulevard "Boulevard") and [parkways](/wiki/Parkway "Parkway"), [thoroughfares](/wiki/Thoroughfare "Thoroughfare") lined with trees, vegetation and greenery. Later projects included the [Bronx River Parkway](/wiki/Bronx_River_Parkway "Bronx River Parkway"), which developed a road while restoring the riverbank and reducing pollution, [Mosholu Parkway](/wiki/Mosholu_Parkway "Mosholu Parkway") and the [Henry Hudson Parkway](/wiki/Henry_Hudson_Parkway "Henry Hudson Parkway").

In 2006, a five-year, $220-million program of capital improvements and natural restoration in 70 Bronx parks was begun (financed by water and sewer revenues) as part of an agreement that allowed a [water filtration plant](/wiki/Croton_Water_Filtration_Plant "Croton Water Filtration Plant") under [Mosholu Golf Course](/wiki/Mosholu_Golf_Course "Mosholu Golf Course") in Van Cortlandt Park. One major focus is on opening more of the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River")'s banks and restoring them to a natural state.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

### Adjacent counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Adjacent counties")]

The Bronx adjoins:[[122]](#cite_note-122)

* [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") – north
* [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York") – southeast (across the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"))
* [Queens County (Queens)](/wiki/Queens "Queens") – south (across the East River)
* [New York County (Manhattan)](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") – southwest
* [Bergen County, New Jersey](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey") – west (across the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"))

## Divisions of the Bronx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Divisions of the Bronx")]

### Regional divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Regional divisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Aerial_view_of_the_Bronx%2C_Harlem_River%2C_Harlem%2C_Hudson_River%2C_George_Washington_Bridge%2C_2008-05-10.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_the_Bronx%2C_Harlem_River%2C_Harlem%2C_Hudson_River%2C_George_Washington_Bridge%2C_2008-05-10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_the_Bronx,_Harlem_River,_Harlem,_Hudson_River,_George_Washington_Bridge,_2008-05-10.jpg)

An aerial view of the Bronx, [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River"), [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem"), [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") and [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge")

There are two primary systems for dividing the Bronx into regions, which do not necessarily agree with one another. One system is based on the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River"), while the other strictly separates [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx") from the rest of the borough.

The [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") divides the borough nearly in half, putting the earlier-settled, more urban, and hillier sections in the west and the newer, more suburban coastal sections in the east. It is an accurate reflection on the Bronx's history considering that the towns that existed in the area prior to annexation to the City of New York generally did not straddle the Bronx River.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In addition, what is today the Bronx was annexed to New York City in two stages: areas west of the Bronx River were annexed in 1874, while areas to the east of the river were annexed in 1895.[[40]](#cite_note-WhyBronx-40)

* **[West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx")**: all parts of the Bronx west of the **Bronx River** (as opposed to Jerome Avenue – this street is simply the "east-west" divider for designating numbered streets as "east" or "west". As the Bronx's numbered streets continue from Manhattan to south, on which the street numbering system is based, Jerome Avenue actually represents a longitudinal halfway point for Manhattan, not the Bronx.)[[123]](#cite_note-geo-123)
* **[East Bronx](/wiki/East_Bronx "East Bronx")**: all parts of the Bronx east of the **Bronx River** (as opposed to Jerome Avenue)[[123]](#cite_note-geo-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

Under this system, the Bronx can be further divided into the following regions:

* **Northwest Bronx**: the northern half of the West Bronx; the area north of Fordham Road and west of the Bronx River
* **Southwest Bronx**: the southern half of the West Bronx; the area south of Fordham Road and west of the Bronx River
* **Northeast Bronx**: the northern half of the East Bronx; the area north of Pelham Parkway and east of the Bronx River
* **Southeast Bronx**: the southern half of the East Bronx; the area south of Pelham Parkway and east of the Bronx River

A second system divides the borough first and foremost into the following sections:

* **[North Bronx](/wiki/North_Bronx "North Bronx")**: all areas not in the South Bronx (Southwest Bronx) – i.e. the Northwest Bronx, Northeast Bronx, and Southeast Bronx
* **[South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx")**: the Southwest Bronx – south of Fordham Road and west of the Bronx River. This includes the areas traditionally considered part of the South Bronx.

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

See also: [List of Bronx neighborhoods](/wiki/List_of_Bronx_neighborhoods "List of Bronx neighborhoods"), [Bronx Community Board](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board "Bronx Community Board"), and [Timeline of town creation in Downstate New York](/wiki/Timeline_of_town_creation_in_Downstate_New_York "Timeline of town creation in Downstate New York")

The number, locations, and boundaries of the Bronx's neighborhoods (many of them sitting on the sites of 19th-century villages) have become unclear with time and successive waves of newcomers. Even city officials do not necessarily agree. In a 2006 article for *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, Manny Fernandez described the disagreement:

> According to a [Department of City Planning](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_City_Planning "New York City Department of City Planning") map of the city's neighborhoods, the Bronx has 49. The map publisher [Hagstrom](/wiki/Hagstrom "Hagstrom") identifies 69. The borough president, [Adolfo Carrión Jr.](/wiki/Adolfo_Carri%C3%B3n_Jr. "Adolfo Carrión Jr."), says 61. The Mayor's Community Assistance Unit, in a listing of the [borough's community boards](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board "Bronx Community Board"), names 68.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

Major neighborhoods of the Bronx include the following.

### East Bronx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: East Bronx")]

Main article: [East Bronx](/wiki/East_Bronx "East Bronx")

([Bronx Community Districts](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board "Bronx Community Board") 9 *[south central]*, 10 *[east]*, 11 *[east central]* and 12 *[north central]*)[[126]](#cite_note-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Co-op_City_Hutch_River.jpg/250px-Co-op_City_Hutch_River.jpg)](/wiki/File:Co-op_City_Hutch_River.jpg)

The neighborhood of [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City "Co-op City") is the largest cooperative housing development in the world.

East of the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River"), the borough is relatively flat and includes four large low peninsulas, or 'necks,' of low-lying land which jut into the waters of the East River and were once saltmarsh: Hunts Point, Clason's Point, Screvin's Neck (Castle Hill Point) and [Throgs Neck](/wiki/Throgs_Neck "Throgs Neck"). The East Bronx has older tenement buildings, low income public housing complexes, and multifamily homes, as well as single family homes. It includes New York City's largest park: [Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park") along the [Westchester](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York")-Bronx border.

Neighborhoods include: [Clason's Point](/wiki/Clason%27s_Point,_Bronx "Clason's Point, Bronx"), [Harding Park](/wiki/Harding_Park,_Bronx "Harding Park, Bronx"), [Soundview](/wiki/Soundview,_Bronx "Soundview, Bronx"), [Castle Hill](/wiki/Castle_Hill,_Bronx "Castle Hill, Bronx"), [Parkchester](/wiki/Parkchester,_Bronx "Parkchester, Bronx") *([Community District 9](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_9 "Bronx Community Board 9"))*; [Throggs Neck](/wiki/Throggs_Neck "Throggs Neck"), [Country Club](/wiki/Country_Club,_Bronx "Country Club, Bronx"), [City Island](/wiki/City_Island,_Bronx "City Island, Bronx"), [Pelham Bay](/wiki/Pelham_Bay "Pelham Bay"), [Edgewater Park](/wiki/Edgewater_Park_(Bronx) "Edgewater Park (Bronx)"), [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City,_Bronx "Co-op City, Bronx") *([Community District 10](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_10 "Bronx Community Board 10"))*; [Westchester Square](/wiki/Westchester_Square,_Bronx "Westchester Square, Bronx"), [Van Nest](/wiki/Van_Nest "Van Nest"), [Pelham Parkway](/wiki/Pelham_Parkway,_Bronx "Pelham Parkway, Bronx"), [Morris Park](/wiki/Morris_Park,_Bronx "Morris Park, Bronx") *([Community District 11](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_11 "Bronx Community Board 11"))*; [Williamsbridge](/wiki/Williamsbridge,_Bronx "Williamsbridge, Bronx"), [Eastchester](/wiki/Eastchester,_Bronx "Eastchester, Bronx"), [Baychester](/wiki/Baychester,_Bronx "Baychester, Bronx"), [Edenwald](/wiki/Edenwald,_Bronx "Edenwald, Bronx") and [Wakefield](/wiki/Wakefield,_Bronx "Wakefield, Bronx") *([Community District 12](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_12 "Bronx Community Board 12"))*.

#### City Island and Hart Island

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: City Island and Hart Island")]

Main articles: [City Island, Bronx](/wiki/City_Island,_Bronx "City Island, Bronx") and [Hart Island (Bronx)](/wiki/Hart_Island_(Bronx) "Hart Island (Bronx)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/CityIsland.jpg/250px-CityIsland.jpg)](/wiki/File:CityIsland.jpg)

A sunken boat off the shore of [City Island](/wiki/City_Island,_Bronx "City Island, Bronx")

([Bronx Community District](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board "Bronx Community Board") 10)

City Island is east of [Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park") in [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") and is known for its seafood restaurants and private waterfront homes.[[127]](#cite_note-127) City Island's single shopping street, City Island Avenue, is reminiscent of a small New England town. It is connected to [Rodman's Neck](/wiki/Rodman%27s_Neck "Rodman's Neck") on the mainland by the [City Island Bridge](/wiki/City_Island_Bridge "City Island Bridge").

East of City Island is [Hart Island](/wiki/Hart_Island_(New_York) "Hart Island (New York)"), which is uninhabited and not open to the public. It once served as a prison and now houses New York City's [potter's field](/wiki/Potter%27s_field "Potter's field") for unclaimed bodies.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### West Bronx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: West Bronx")]

Main article: [West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Grand_Conc_165_jeh.JPG/250px-Grand_Conc_165_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Grand_Conc_165_jeh.JPG)

[Grand Concourse](/wiki/Grand_Concourse_(Bronx) "Grand Concourse (Bronx)") at East 165th Street in 2008

([Bronx Community Districts](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board "Bronx Community Board") 1 to 8, progressing roughly from south to northwest)

The western parts of the Bronx are hillier and are dominated by a series of parallel ridges, running south to north. The West Bronx has older apartment buildings, low income public housing complexes, multifamily homes in its lower income areas as well as larger single family homes in more affluent areas such as [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx") and [Fieldston](/wiki/Fieldston,_Bronx "Fieldston, Bronx").[[129]](#cite_note-129) It includes New York City's third-largest park: [Van Cortlandt Park](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park") along the Westchester-Bronx border. The [Grand Concourse](/wiki/Grand_Concourse_(Bronx) "Grand Concourse (Bronx)"), a wide boulevard, runs through it, north to south.

#### Northwestern Bronx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Northwestern Bronx")]

([Bronx Community Districts](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board "Bronx Community Board") 7 *[between the [Bronx](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") and [Harlem Rivers](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River")]* and 8 *[facing the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River")]* – plus part of Board 12)

Neighborhoods include: Fordham-Bedford, [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_Bronx "Bedford Park, Bronx"), [Norwood](/wiki/Norwood,_Bronx "Norwood, Bronx"), [Kingsbridge Heights](/wiki/Kingsbridge_Heights "Kingsbridge Heights") *([Community District 7](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_7 "Bronx Community Board 7"))*, [Kingsbridge](/wiki/Kingsbridge,_Bronx "Kingsbridge, Bronx"), [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx") *([Community District 8](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_8 "Bronx Community Board 8"))*, and [Woodlawn Heights](/wiki/Woodlawn_Heights,_Bronx "Woodlawn Heights, Bronx") *([Community District 12](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_12 "Bronx Community Board 12"))*.
([Marble Hill, Manhattan](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan") is now connected by land to the Bronx rather than Manhattan and is served by Bronx Community District 8.)

#### South Bronx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: South Bronx")]

Main article: [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Morrisheightsbx.JPG/250px-Morrisheightsbx.JPG)](/wiki/File:Morrisheightsbx.JPG)

[Morris Heights](/wiki/Morris_Heights,_Bronx "Morris Heights, Bronx"), a Bronx neighborhood of over 45,000

([Bronx Community Districts](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board "Bronx Community Board") 1 to 6 plus part of CD 7—*progressing northwards, CDs 2, 3 and 6 border the [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") from its mouth to [Bronx Park](/wiki/Bronx_Park "Bronx Park"), while 1, 4, 5 and 7 face Manhattan across the [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River")*)

Like other [neighborhoods in New York City](/wiki/Neighborhoods_in_New_York_City "Neighborhoods in New York City"), the South Bronx has no official boundaries. The name has been used to represent poverty in the Bronx and is applied to progressively more northern places so that by the 2000s, [Fordham Road](/wiki/Fordham_Road "Fordham Road") was often used as a northern limit. The [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River") more consistently forms an eastern boundary. The South Bronx has many high-density apartment buildings, low income public housing complexes, and multi-unit homes. The South Bronx is home to the [Bronx County Courthouse](/wiki/Bronx_County_Courthouse "Bronx County Courthouse"), Borough Hall, and other government buildings, as well as [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium"). The [Cross Bronx Expressway](/wiki/Cross_Bronx_Expressway "Cross Bronx Expressway") bisects it, east to west. The South Bronx has some of the poorest neighborhoods in the country, as well as very high crime areas.

Neighborhoods include: [The Hub](/wiki/The_Hub,_Bronx "The Hub, Bronx") (a retail district at [Third Avenue](/wiki/Third_Avenue "Third Avenue") and East 149th Street), [Port Morris](/wiki/Port_Morris,_Bronx "Port Morris, Bronx"), [Mott Haven](/wiki/Mott_Haven,_Bronx "Mott Haven, Bronx") *([Community District 1](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_1 "Bronx Community Board 1"))*, [Melrose](/wiki/Melrose,_Bronx "Melrose, Bronx") *([Community District 1](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_1 "Bronx Community Board 1") & [Community District 3](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_3 "Bronx Community Board 3"))*, [Morrisania](/wiki/Morrisania "Morrisania"), [East Morrisania](/wiki/East_Morrisania,_Bronx "East Morrisania, Bronx") [also known as Crotona Park East] *([Community District 3](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_3 "Bronx Community Board 3"))*, [Hunts Point](/wiki/Hunts_Point,_Bronx "Hunts Point, Bronx"), [Longwood](/wiki/Longwood,_Bronx "Longwood, Bronx") *([Community District 2](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_2 "Bronx Community Board 2"))*, [Highbridge](/wiki/Highbridge,_Bronx "Highbridge, Bronx"), [Concourse](/wiki/Concourse,_Bronx "Concourse, Bronx") *([Community District 4](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_4 "Bronx Community Board 4"))*, [West Farms](/wiki/West_Farms,_Bronx "West Farms, Bronx"), [Belmont](/wiki/Belmont,_Bronx "Belmont, Bronx"), [East Tremont](/wiki/East_Tremont,_Bronx "East Tremont, Bronx") *([Community District 6](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_6 "Bronx Community Board 6"))*, [Tremont](/wiki/Tremont,_Bronx "Tremont, Bronx"), [Morris Heights](/wiki/Morris_Heights,_Bronx "Morris Heights, Bronx") *([Community District 5](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_5 "Bronx Community Board 5"))*, [University Heights](/wiki/University_Heights,_Bronx "University Heights, Bronx"). *([Community District 5](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_5 "Bronx Community Board 5") & [Community District 7](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_7 "Bronx Community Board 7"))*.

Ethnic origins in the Bronx

#### 2018 estimates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 2018 estimates")]

The borough's most populous racial group, White, declined from 99.3% in 1920 to 14.9% in 2018.[[138]](#cite_note-:1-138)

The Bronx has 532,487 housing units, with a median value of $371,800, and with an owner-occupancy rate of 19.7%, the lowest of the five boroughs. There are 495,356 households, with 2.85 persons per household. 59.3% of residents speak a language besides English at home, the highest rate of the five boroughs.

In the Bronx, the population is 7.2% under 5, 17.6% 6–18, 62.4% 19–64, and 12.8% over 65. 52.9% of the population is female. 35.3% of residents are foreign born.

The per capita income is $19,721, while the median household income is $36,593, both being the lowest of the five boroughs. 27.9% of residents live below the poverty line, the highest of the five boroughs.

#### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

According to the 2010 Census, 53.5% of Bronx's population was of Hispanic, Latino, or Spanish origin (they may be of any race); 30.1% non-Hispanic Black or African American, 10.9% of the population was non-Hispanic White, 3.4% non-Hispanic Asian, 1.2% of two or more races (non-Hispanic), and 0.6% from some other race (non-Hispanic).

As of 2010, 46.29% (584,463) of Bronx residents aged five and older spoke [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") at home, while 44.02% (555,767) spoke [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), 2.48% (31,361) [African languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Africa "Languages of Africa"), 0.91% (11,455) [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), 0.90% (11,355) [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), 0.87% (10,946) [various Indic languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"), 0.70% (8,836) [other Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), and [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") was spoken at home by 0.50% (6,610) of the population over the age of five. In total, 55.98% (706,783) of the Bronx's population age five and older spoke a language at home other than English.[[140]](#cite_note-140) A [Garifuna](/wiki/Garifuna_language "Garifuna language")-speaking community from [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras") and [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") also makes the Bronx its home.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_New_York_City_%285559914315%29.png/250px-Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_New_York_City_%285559914315%29.png)](/wiki/File:Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_New_York_City_(5559914315).png)

#### 2009 community survey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: 2009 community survey")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/The_Bronx "Special:EditPage/The Bronx") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(June 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The Bronx is the only New York City borough with a [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") majority,[[142]](#cite_note-quickfacts-census-142) many of whom are [Puerto Ricans](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_American "Puerto Rican American") and [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_American "Dominican American").[[143]](#cite_note-143) According to the 2009 American Community Survey, [Black Americans](/wiki/Black_Americans "Black Americans") were the second largest [racial/ethnic](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census") group in the Bronx. Black people of both Hispanic and non-Hispanic origin represented over one-third (35.4%) of the Bronx's population. Black people of non-Hispanic origin made up 30.8% of the population. Over 495,200 Black people resided in the borough, of whom 87% were non-Hispanic. Over 61,000 people identified themselves as Sub-Saharan African in the survey, making up 4.4% of the population.[[144]](#cite_note-:3-144)

Multiracial Americans are also a sizable minority in the Bronx. People of multiracial heritage number over 41,800 individuals and represent 3.0% of the population. People of mixed [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") and [European American](/wiki/European_Americans "European Americans") heritage number over 6,850 members and form 0.5% of the population. People of mixed [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_people_of_North_America "Indigenous people of North America") and European heritage number over 2,450 members and form 0.2% of the population. People of mixed [Asian](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American") and European heritage number over 880 members and form 0.1% of the population. People of mixed African American and Native American heritage number over 1,220 members and form 0.1% of the population.[[144]](#cite_note-:3-144)

Out of all five boroughs, the Bronx has the lowest number and proportion of white residents. As of 2009, [White Americans](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans") of both Hispanic and non-Hispanic origin represented over one-fifth (22.9%) of the Bronx's population, or 320,640 people. [Non-Hispanic White](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_White "Non-Hispanic White") people accounted for one-eighth of the population (12.1%, or 168,570 12.1%). This is in contrast to a century ago, when almost all Bronx residents were white (99.3% in 1920). That share fell to about one-third by 1980 (34.4%).[[145]](#cite_note-census-145) As of 2009, White Americans of both Hispanic and non-Hispanic origin represented one-fifth (22.9%) of the Bronx's population, but counting non-Hispanic White people the proportion was under one-eighth (12.1%). The majority of the non-Hispanic European American population is of Italian and Irish descent. [People of Italian descent](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") numbered over 55,000 individuals and made up 3.9% of the population. [People of Irish descent](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") numbered over 43,500 individuals and made up 3.1% of the population. [German Americans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") and [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") made up 1.4% and 0.8% of the population respectively. The Bronx has the largest [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_Americans "Albanian Americans") community in the United States.[[146]](#cite_note-146) As of 2018, non-Hispanic White people account for about one in seven residents (14.9% in 2018).[[138]](#cite_note-:1-138)

### Population and housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Population and housing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Bronxpoverty.JPG/250px-Bronxpoverty.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bronxpoverty.JPG)

**Poverty concentrations** within the Bronx, by Census Tract

As of the 2010 census, there were 1,385,108 people living in the Bronx, a 3.9% increase since 2000.

As of the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"),[[137]](#cite_note-GR2-137) there were 1,332,650 people, 463,212 households, and 314,984 families residing in the borough. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 31,709.3 inhabitants per square mile (12,243.0 inhabitants/km2). There were 490,659 housing units at an average density of 11,674.8 units per square mile (4,507.7 units/km2).[[137]](#cite_note-GR2-137) Census estimates place total population of Bronx county at 1,392,002 as of 2012.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

There were 463,212 households, out of which 38.1% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 31.4% were married couples living together, 30.4% had a female householder with no husband present, and 32.0% were non-families. 27.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 9.4% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.78 and the average family size was 3.37.[[137]](#cite_note-GR2-137)

The age distribution of the population in the Bronx were as follows: 29.8% under the age of 18, 10.6% from 18 to 24, 30.7% from 25 to 44, 18.8% from 45 to 64, and 10.1% 65 years of age or older. The median age was 31 years. For every 100 females, there were 87.0 males.[[137]](#cite_note-GR2-137)

### Individual and household income

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Individual and household income")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(April 2017)* |

The 1999 [median income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") for a household in the borough was $27,611, and the median family income was $30,682. Men had a median income of $31,178 versus $29,429 for women. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the borough was $13,959. About 28.0% of families and 30.7% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 41.5% of those under age 18 and 21.3% of those age 65 or over. More than half of the neighborhoods in the Bronx are high poverty or extreme poverty areas.[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151)

From 2015 census data, the [median income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") for a household was (in 2015 dollars) $34,299. [Per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") in past 12 months (in 2015 dollars): $18,456 with persons in poverty at 30.3%. Per the 2016 Census data, the [median income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") for a household was $35,302. Per capita income was cited at $18,896.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)
