### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sports")]

See also: [Sports in the New York metropolitan area](/wiki/Sports_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Sports in the New York metropolitan area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Babe_Ruth_Bows_Out.jpg/330px-Babe_Ruth_Bows_Out.jpg)](/wiki/File:Babe_Ruth_Bows_Out.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Yankee_Stadium_upper_deck_2010.jpg/330px-Yankee_Stadium_upper_deck_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yankee_Stadium_upper_deck_2010.jpg)

[Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth"), often considered the greatest baseball player of all time, is seen retiring from the [Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") in a [Pulitzer prize-winning photograph](/wiki/Babe_Ruth_Bows_Out "Babe Ruth Bows Out") in the Bronx's original [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium_(1923) "Yankee Stadium (1923)") ([current field](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium") at right).

The Bronx is the home of the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees")—nicknamed "the Bronx Bombers"—of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball").[[154]](#cite_note-154) The Yankees have won 27 [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") titles, more than any other team, and their roster has featured players like [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth"), [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig"), [Joe DiMaggio](/wiki/Joe_DiMaggio "Joe DiMaggio"), and [Mickey Mantle](/wiki/Mickey_Mantle "Mickey Mantle").[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156)

When the team's original [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium_(1923) "Yankee Stadium (1923)") opened in 1923, it was the largest baseball park.[[157]](#cite_note-157) The field also hosted college football games, and was the home of two [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") teams, the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees_(NFL) "New York Yankees (NFL)") (1926–1929) and the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") (1956–1973).[[158]](#cite_note-158) In 2008, the park was replaced with the current [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium").[[159]](#cite_note-159)

The Bronx additionally hosts the only [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") team in the five boroughs, the [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC"), which also plays in Yankee Stadium.[[160]](#cite_note-160) Part of the [New York City Marathon](/wiki/New_York_City_Marathon "New York City Marathon") travels through the Bronx, including the notoriously difficult Mile 20.[[161]](#cite_note-161) From 1889 to 1904, the borough used to have a horse racing facility, the [Morris Park Racecourse](/wiki/Morris_Park_Racecourse "Morris Park Racecourse").[[162]](#cite_note-162) In its later years, the course was used for [motor racing](/wiki/1905_Morris_Park_5 "1905 Morris Park 5"): a new [land speed record](/wiki/Land_speed_record "Land speed record") was reached on the track.[[163]](#cite_note-163) College teams in the Bronx include the [Fordham Rams](/wiki/Fordham_Rams "Fordham Rams") and the [Lehman Lightning](/wiki/Lehman_Lightning "Lehman Lightning").[[164]](#cite_note-164)[[165]](#cite_note-165)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Music")]

See also: [Music of New York City](/wiki/Music_of_New_York_City "Music of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Dj_Kool_Herc-03.jpg/250px-Dj_Kool_Herc-03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dj_Kool_Herc-03.jpg)

[DJ Kool Herc](/wiki/DJ_Kool_Herc "DJ Kool Herc") in 1999

The Bronx has had a long association with music. In the early 20th century, it was a center for the evolution of [Latin jazz](/wiki/Latin_jazz "Latin jazz").[[166]](#cite_note-166)[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-168) The [Bronx Opera](/wiki/Bronx_Opera "Bronx Opera") was established in 1967.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

In the 1970s, the Bronx was strongly associated with the development of [hip hop music](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"),[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171) which evolved during a decade of demographic and economic decline for the borough.[[172]](#cite_note-172) One of the genre's pioneers, [DJ Kool Herc](/wiki/DJ_Kool_Herc "DJ Kool Herc"), held parties in the community room of an apartment building at 1520 [Sedgwick Avenue](/wiki/Sedgwick_Avenue "Sedgwick Avenue"), where he experimented with [turntablist](/wiki/Turntablism "Turntablism") techniques such as [mixing](/wiki/DJ_mix "DJ mix") and [scratching](/wiki/Scratching "Scratching") of [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") records, as well as [rapping](/wiki/Rapping "Rapping") during extended instrumentals.[[173]](#cite_note-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-PBS-175) Other significant Bronx DJs from this period include [Grandmaster Flash](/wiki/Grandmaster_Flash "Grandmaster Flash") and [Afrika Bambaataa](/wiki/Afrika_Bambaataa "Afrika Bambaataa").[[176]](#cite_note-176) In addition, the Bronx was an important base for [drill culture](/wiki/Drill_music "Drill music"), with the rise of rappers such as [Kay Flock](/wiki/Kay_Flock "Kay Flock"), [Sha EK](/wiki/Sha_EK "Sha EK") and many others.

### Off-Off-Broadway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Off-Off-Broadway")]

Main article: [Off-Off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Off-Broadway "Off-Off-Broadway")

The Bronx is home to several [Off-Off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Off-Broadway "Off-Off-Broadway") theaters, many staging new works by immigrant playwrights from Latin America and Africa. The [Pregones Theater](/wiki/Pregones_Theater "Pregones Theater"), which produces Latin American work, opened a new 130-seat theater in 2005 on Walton Avenue in the South Bronx. Some artists from elsewhere in New York City have begun to converge on the area, and housing prices have nearly quadrupled in the area since 2002. However, rising prices directly correlate to a housing shortage across the city and the entire metro area.

### Arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Arts")]

The [Bronx Academy of Arts and Dance](/wiki/Bronx_Academy_of_Arts_and_Dance "Bronx Academy of Arts and Dance"), founded in 1998 by [Arthur Aviles](/wiki/Arthur_Aviles "Arthur Aviles") and Charles Rice-Gonzalez, provides dance, theatre and art workshops, festivals and performances focusing on contemporary and modern art in relation to race, gender and sexuality. It is home to the Arthur Aviles Typical Theatre, a contemporary dance company, and the Bronx Dance Coalition. The academy was formerly in the [American Bank Note Company Building](/wiki/American_Bank_Note_Company_Building "American Bank Note Company Building") before relocating to a venue on the grounds of St. Peter's Episcopal Church.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

The [Bronx Museum of the Arts](/wiki/Bronx_Museum_of_the_Arts "Bronx Museum of the Arts"), founded in 1971, exhibits 20th century and contemporary art through its central museum space and 11,000 square feet (1,000 m2) of galleries. Many of its exhibitions are on themes of special interest to the Bronx. Its permanent collection features more than 800 works of art, primarily by artists from Africa, Asia and Latin America, including paintings, photographs, prints, drawings, and mixed media. The museum was temporarily closed in 2006 while it underwent an expansion designed by the architectural firm [Arquitectonica](/wiki/Arquitectonica "Arquitectonica") that would double the museum's size to 33,000 square feet (3,100 m2).[[178]](#cite_note-178)

The Bronx has also become home to a peculiar poetic tribute in the form of the "[Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine") Memorial", better known as the [Lorelei Fountain](/wiki/Lorelei_Fountain "Lorelei Fountain"). After Heine's German birthplace of [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf") had rejected, allegedly for [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitic "Antisemitic") motives, a centennial monument to the radical [German-Jewish](/wiki/German-Jewish "German-Jewish") poet (1797–1856), his incensed [German-American](/wiki/German-American "German-American") admirers, including [Carl Schurz](/wiki/Carl_Schurz "Carl Schurz"), started a movement to place one instead in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), at [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") and 59th Street. However, this intention was thwarted by a combination of ethnic antagonism, aesthetic controversy and political struggles over the institutional control of public art.[[179]](#cite_note-GrayNYT2007-179) In 1899, the memorial by [Ernst Gustav Herter](/wiki/Ernst_Gustav_Herter "Ernst Gustav Herter") was placed in [Joyce Kilmer Park](/wiki/Joyce_Kilmer_Park "Joyce Kilmer Park"), near the [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium"). In 1999, it was moved to 161st Street and the Concourse.

### Maritime heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Maritime heritage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Bronx_Zoo_-_NY_-_USA_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Bronx_Zoo_-_NY_-_USA_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronx_Zoo_-_NY_-_USA_-_panoramio.jpg)

The [Bronx Zoo](/wiki/Bronx_Zoo "Bronx Zoo") is the largest zoo in New York City, and among the largest in the country.

The [peninsular](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") borough's maritime heritage is acknowledged in several ways. The City Island Historical Society and Nautical Museum occupies a former public school designed by the New York City school system's turn-of-the-last-century master architect [C. B. J. Snyder](/wiki/C._B._J._Snyder "C. B. J. Snyder"). The state's [Maritime College](/wiki/SUNY_Maritime_College "SUNY Maritime College") in [Fort Schuyler](/wiki/Fort_Schuyler "Fort Schuyler") (on the southeastern shore) houses the [Maritime Industry Museum](/wiki/Maritime_Industry_Museum "Maritime Industry Museum").[[180]](#cite_note-180) In addition, the Harlem River is reemerging as ["Scullers' Row"](/wiki/Sculling "Sculling")[[181]](#cite_note-181) due in large part to the efforts of the Bronx River Restoration Project,[[182]](#cite_note-182) a joint public-private endeavor of the city's parks department. [Canoeing](/wiki/Canoeing "Canoeing") and [kayaking](/wiki/Kayaking "Kayaking") on the borough's namesake river have been promoted by the Bronx River Alliance. The river is also straddled by the [New York Botanical Gardens](/wiki/New_York_Botanical_Gardens "New York Botanical Gardens"), its neighbor, the [Bronx Zoo](/wiki/Bronx_Zoo "Bronx Zoo"), and a little further south, on the west shore, Bronx River Art Center.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

### Community celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Community celebrations")]

"Bronx Week", traditionally held in May, began as a one-day celebration. Begun by Bronx historian Lloyd Ultan and supported by then borough president Robert Abrams, the original one-day program was based on the "Bronx Borough Day" festival which took place in the 1920s. The following year, at the height of the decade's civil unrest, the festival was extended to a one-week event. In the 1980s the key event, the "Bronx Ball", was launched. The week includes the Bronx Week Parade as well as inductions into the "Bronx Walk of Fame".[[184]](#cite_note-184)

Various Bronx neighborhoods conduct their own community celebrations. The Arthur Avenue "Little Italy" neighborhood conducts an annual Autumn Ferragosto Festival that celebrates Italian culture.[[185]](#cite_note-185) [Hunts Point](/wiki/Hunts_Point,_Bronx "Hunts Point, Bronx") hosts an annual "Fish Parade and Summer Festival" at the start of summer.[[186]](#cite_note-186) [Edgewater Park](/wiki/Edgewater_Park_(Bronx) "Edgewater Park (Bronx)") hosts an annual "Ragamuffin" children's walk in November.[[187]](#cite_note-187) There are several events to honor the borough's veterans.[[188]](#cite_note-188) Albanian Independence Day is also observed.[[189]](#cite_note-189)

There are also parades to celebrate Dominican, Italian, and Irish heritage.[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191)[[192]](#cite_note-192)

### Press and broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Press and broadcasting")]

The Bronx is home to several local newspapers and radio and television studios.

#### Newspapers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Newspapers")]

The Bronx has several local newspapers, including The Bronx Daily, *The [Bronx News](/wiki/Bronx_News "Bronx News")*,[[193]](#cite_note-193) *[Parkchester](/wiki/Parkchester "Parkchester") News*, *City News*, *The [Norwood News](/wiki/Norwood_News "Norwood News")*, *The [Riverdale Press](/wiki/Riverdale_Press "Riverdale Press")*, *[Riverdale Review](/wiki/Riverdale_Review "Riverdale Review")*, *The [Bronx Times Reporter](/wiki/Bronx_Times_Reporter "Bronx Times Reporter")*, and *[Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City "Co-op City") Times*. Four non-profit news outlets, *[Norwood News](/wiki/Norwood_News "Norwood News")*, *Mount Hope Monitor*, *[Mott Haven Herald](/wiki/Mott_Haven_Herald "Mott Haven Herald")* and *The [Hunts Point](/wiki/Hunts_Point,_Bronx "Hunts Point, Bronx") Express* serve the borough's poorer communities. The editor and co-publisher of *The Riverdale Press*, Bernard Stein, won the [Pulitzer Prize for Editorial Writing](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Editorial_Writing "Pulitzer Prize for Editorial Writing") for his editorials about Bronx and New York City issues in 1998. (Stein graduated from the [Bronx High School of Science](/wiki/Bronx_High_School_of_Science "Bronx High School of Science") in 1959.)

The Bronx once had its own daily newspaper, *[The Bronx Home News](/wiki/The_Bronx_Home_News "The Bronx Home News")*, which started publishing on January 20, 1907, and merged into the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")* in 1948. It became a special section of the *Post*, sold only in the Bronx, and eventually disappeared from view.

#### Radio and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Radio and television")]

One of New York City's major non-commercial radio broadcasters is [WFUV](/wiki/WFUV "WFUV"), a [National Public Radio](/wiki/National_Public_Radio "National Public Radio")-affiliated 50,000-watt station broadcasting from [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University")'s Rose Hill campus in the Bronx. The radio station's antenna was relocated to the top of an apartment building owned by [Montefiore Medical Center](/wiki/Montefiore_Medical_Center "Montefiore Medical Center"), which expanded the reach of the station's signal.[[194]](#cite_note-194)

The City of New York has an official television station run by [NYC Media](/wiki/NYC_Media "NYC Media") and broadcasting from [Bronx Community College](/wiki/Bronx_Community_College "Bronx Community College"), and [Cablevision](/wiki/Cablevision "Cablevision") operates [News 12 The Bronx](/wiki/News_12_Networks "News 12 Networks"), both of which feature programming based in the Bronx. [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City "Co-op City") was the first area in the Bronx, and the first in New York beyond [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), to have its own [cable television](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") provider. The local [public-access television](/wiki/Public-access_television "Public-access television") station [BronxNet](/wiki/BronxNet "BronxNet") originates from Herbert H. Lehman College, the borough's only four year CUNY school, and provides [government-access television](/wiki/Government-access_television "Government-access television") (GATV) public affairs programming in addition to programming produced by Bronx residents.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Economy of New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City")

Shopping malls and markets in the Bronx include:

* [Bay Plaza Shopping Center](/wiki/Bay_Plaza_Shopping_Center "Bay Plaza Shopping Center")
* [Bronx Terminal Market](/wiki/Bronx_Terminal_Market "Bronx Terminal Market")
* [Hunts Point Cooperative Market](/wiki/Hunts_Point_Cooperative_Market "Hunts Point Cooperative Market")

### Shopping districts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Shopping districts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/The_Hub_-_East_149th_Street%2C_The_Bronx.jpg/250px-The_Hub_-_East_149th_Street%2C_The_Bronx.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Hub_-_East_149th_Street,_The_Bronx.jpg)

[The Hub](/wiki/The_Hub,_Bronx "The Hub, Bronx") on [Third Avenue](/wiki/Third_Avenue "Third Avenue")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/BTM_149_jeh.jpg/250px-BTM_149_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:BTM_149_jeh.jpg)

Renovated Prow Building, part of the original [Bronx Terminal Market](/wiki/Bronx_Terminal_Market "Bronx Terminal Market")

Prominent shopping areas in the Bronx include [Fordham Road](/wiki/Fordham_Road "Fordham Road"), [Bay Plaza](/wiki/Bay_Plaza_Shopping_Center "Bay Plaza Shopping Center") in [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City "Co-op City"), [The Hub](/wiki/The_Hub,_Bronx "The Hub, Bronx"), the [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx")/[Kingsbridge](/wiki/Kingsbridge,_Bronx "Kingsbridge, Bronx") shopping center, and [Bruckner Boulevard](/wiki/Bruckner_Boulevard "Bruckner Boulevard"). Shops are also concentrated on streets aligned underneath elevated railroad lines, including Westchester Avenue, [White Plains Road](/wiki/White_Plains_Road "White Plains Road"), [Jerome Avenue](/wiki/Jerome_Avenue "Jerome Avenue"), [Southern Boulevard](/wiki/Southern_Boulevard_(Bronx) "Southern Boulevard (Bronx)"), and [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Bronx) "Broadway (Bronx)"). The [Bronx Terminal Market](/wiki/Bronx_Terminal_Market "Bronx Terminal Market") contains several [big-box stores](/wiki/Big-box_store "Big-box store"), which opened in 2009 south of Yankee Stadium.

The Bronx has three primary shopping centers: The Hub, Gateway Center and Southern Boulevard. The Hub–Third Avenue Business Improvement District (B.I.D.), in [The Hub](/wiki/The_Hub,_Bronx "The Hub, Bronx"), is the retail heart of the [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx"), where four roads converge: East 149th Street, Willis, Melrose and Third Avenues.[[196]](#cite_note-196) It is primarily inside the neighborhood of [Melrose](/wiki/Melrose,_Bronx "Melrose, Bronx") but also lines the northern border of [Mott Haven](/wiki/Mott_Haven,_Bronx "Mott Haven, Bronx").[[197]](#cite_note-197) The Hub has been called "the Broadway of the Bronx", being likened to [the real Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Bronx) "Broadway (Bronx)") in Manhattan and the northwestern Bronx.[[198]](#cite_note-198) It is the site of both maximum traffic and architectural density. In configuration, it resembles a miniature [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"), a spatial "bow-tie" created by the geometry of the street.[[199]](#cite_note-199) The Hub is part of [Bronx Community Board 1](/wiki/Bronx_Community_Board_1 "Bronx Community Board 1").

The [Bronx Terminal Market](/wiki/Bronx_Terminal_Market "Bronx Terminal Market"), in the [West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx"), formerly known as Gateway Center, is a shopping center that encompasses less than one million square feet of retail space, built on a 17 acres (7 ha) site that formerly held a wholesale fruit and vegetable market also named Bronx Terminal Market as well as the former [Bronx House of Detention](/wiki/Bronx_House_of_Detention "Bronx House of Detention"), south of [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium"). The $500 million shopping center, which was completed in 2009, saw the construction of new buildings and two smaller buildings, one new and the other a renovation of an existing building that was part of the original market. The two main buildings are linked by a six-level garage for 2,600 cars. The center's design has earned it a [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") "Silver" designation.[[200]](#cite_note-200)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Local government")]

Main article: [Government of New York City](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City")

Since New York City's consolidation in 1898, the New York City Charter that provides for a "strong" [mayor–council system](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government") has governed the Bronx. The centralized New York City government is responsible for public education, correctional institutions, libraries, public safety, recreational facilities, sanitation, water supply, and welfare services in the Bronx.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/85/A_picture_of_Council_Member_Gibson.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_picture_of_Council_Member_Gibson.jpg)

Bronx [borough president](/wiki/Borough_president "Borough president") [Vanessa Gibson](/wiki/Vanessa_Gibson "Vanessa Gibson")

The office of [Borough President](/wiki/Borough_President "Borough President") was created in the consolidation of 1898 to balance centralization with local authority. Each borough president had a powerful administrative role derived from having a vote on the [New York City Board of Estimate](/wiki/New_York_City_Board_of_Estimate "New York City Board of Estimate"), which was responsible for creating and approving the city's budget and proposals for land use. In 1989 the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") declared the Board of Estimate unconstitutional on the grounds that [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), the most populous borough, had no greater effective representation on the Board than [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), the least populous borough, a violation of the [Fourteenth Amendment's](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") [Equal Protection Clause](/wiki/Equal_Protection_Clause "Equal Protection Clause") pursuant to the high court's 1964 "one man, one vote" decision.[[201]](#cite_note-law.cornell.edu-201)

Since 1990, the Borough President has acted as an advocate for the borough at the mayoral agencies, the [City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council"), the New York state government, and corporations.

Until March 1, 2009, the Borough President of the Bronx was [Adolfo Carrión Jr.](/wiki/Adolfo_Carri%C3%B3n_Jr. "Adolfo Carrión Jr."), elected as a [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") in 2001 and 2005 before retiring early to direct the [White House Office of Urban Affairs Policy](/wiki/White_House_Office_of_Urban_Affairs_Policy "White House Office of Urban Affairs Policy"). His successor, Democratic [New York State Assembly](/wiki/New_York_State_Assembly "New York State Assembly") member [Rubén Díaz, Jr.](/wiki/Rub%C3%A9n_D%C3%ADaz_Jr. "Rubén Díaz Jr.") — after winning a special election on April 21, 2009, by a vote of 86.3% (29,420) on the "Bronx Unity" line to 13.3% (4,646) for the Republican district leader Anthony Ribustello on the "People First" line,[[202]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-202)[[203]](#cite_note-vote.nyc.ny.us-203) — became Borough President on May 1, 2009. In 2021, Rubén Díaz's Democratic successor, [Vanessa Gibson](/wiki/Vanessa_Gibson "Vanessa Gibson") was elected (began serving in 2022) with 79.9% of the vote against 13.4% for Janell King (Republican) and 6.5% for Sammy Ravelo (Conservative).

All of the Bronx's currently elected public officials have first won the nomination of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(U.S.) "Democratic Party (U.S.)") (in addition to any other endorsements). Local party platforms center on affordable housing, education and economic development. Controversial political issues in the Bronx include environmental issues, the cost of housing, and annexation of parkland for new [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium").[[204]](#cite_note-204)

Since its separation from [New York County](/wiki/New_York_County "New York County") on January 1, 1914, the Bronx, has had, like each of the other 61 counties of New York State, its own criminal court system[[7]](#cite_note-courtstart-7) and [District Attorney](/wiki/District_attorney "District attorney"), the chief public prosecutor who is directly elected by popular vote. [Darcel D. Clark](/wiki/Darcel_D._Clark "Darcel D. Clark") has been the [Bronx County District Attorney](/wiki/Bronx_County_District_Attorney "Bronx County District Attorney") since 2016. Her predecessor was [Robert T. Johnson](/wiki/Robert_T._Johnson_(lawyer) "Robert T. Johnson (lawyer)"), the District Attorney from 1989 to 2015. He was the first African-American District Attorney in New York State.[[205]](#cite_note-205)

The Bronx also has twelve [Community Boards](/wiki/Community_boards_of_the_Bronx "Community boards of the Bronx"), appointed bodies that advise on land use and municipal facilities and services for local residents, businesses and institutions.

After becoming a separate county in 1914, the Bronx has supported only two Republican presidential candidates. It voted heavily for the winning Republican [Warren G. Harding](/wiki/Warren_G._Harding "Warren G. Harding") in [1920](/wiki/1920_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1920 United States presidential election in New York"), but much more narrowly on a split vote for his victorious Republican successor [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Calvin_Coolidge "Calvin Coolidge") in [1924](/wiki/1924_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1924 United States presidential election in New York") (Coolidge 79,562; [John W. Davis](/wiki/John_W._Davis "John W. Davis"), Dem., 72,834; [Robert La Follette](/wiki/Robert_M._La_Follette,_Sr. "Robert M. La Follette, Sr."), 62,202 equally divided between the [Progressive](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1924) "Progressive Party (United States, 1924)") and [Socialist](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") lines).

Since then, the Bronx has always supported the Democratic Party's nominee for president, starting with a vote of 2–1 for the unsuccessful [Al Smith](/wiki/Al_Smith "Al Smith") in 1928, followed by four 2–1 votes for the successful [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"). (Both had been Governors of New York, but Republican former Gov. [Thomas E. Dewey](/wiki/Thomas_E._Dewey "Thomas E. Dewey") won only 28% of the Bronx's vote in 1948 against 55% for Pres. [Harry Truman](/wiki/Harry_Truman "Harry Truman"), the winning Democrat, and 17% for [Henry A. Wallace](/wiki/Henry_A._Wallace "Henry A. Wallace") of the [Progressives](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1948) "Progressive Party (United States, 1948)"). It was only 32 years earlier, by contrast, that another Republican former Governor who narrowly lost the Presidency, [Charles Evans Hughes](/wiki/Charles_Evans_Hughes "Charles Evans Hughes"), had won 42.6% of the Bronx's 1916 vote against Democratic President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson")'s 49.8% and Socialist candidate [Allan Benson](/wiki/Allan_Benson "Allan Benson")'s 7.3%.)[[210]](#cite_note-210) [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") improved on the Republican Party's performance from a historic low of 8% in 2012 to 27% in 2024 over the course of his three runs for president, the highest for Republicans since [1984](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1984 United States presidential election in New York").

### Federal Representatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Federal Representatives")]

As of 2025, four Democrats represented the Bronx in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"):[[211]](#cite_note-govtrack.us_2018-211)

* [Adriano Espaillat](/wiki/Adriano_Espaillat "Adriano Espaillat") (first elected in 2016) represents [New York's 13th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_13th_congressional_district "New York's 13th congressional district"), which includes the Bronx neighborhoods of [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_Bronx "Bedford Park, Bronx"), [Jerome Park](/wiki/Jerome_Park,_Bronx "Jerome Park, Bronx"), [Kingsbridge Heights](/wiki/Kingsbridge_Heights,_Bronx "Kingsbridge Heights, Bronx"), [Norwood](/wiki/Norwood,_Bronx "Norwood, Bronx"), and parts of [Fordham](/wiki/Fordham,_Bronx "Fordham, Bronx"), [Kingsbridge](/wiki/Kingsbridge,_Bronx "Kingsbridge, Bronx"), [Morris Heights](/wiki/Morris_Heights,_Bronx "Morris Heights, Bronx"), and [University Heights](/wiki/University_Heights,_Bronx "University Heights, Bronx"), as well as a portion of Manhattan.
* [Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez](/wiki/Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez "Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez") (first elected in 2018) represents [New York's 14th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_14th_congressional_district "New York's 14th congressional district"), which includes the neighborhoods of [City Island](/wiki/City_Island,_Bronx "City Island, Bronx"), [Country Club](/wiki/Country_Club,_Bronx "Country Club, Bronx"), [Van Nest](/wiki/Van_Nest,_Bronx "Van Nest, Bronx"), [Morris Park](/wiki/Morris_Park,_Bronx "Morris Park, Bronx"), [Parkchester](/wiki/Parkchester,_Bronx "Parkchester, Bronx"), [Pelham Bay](/wiki/Pelham_Bay,_Bronx "Pelham Bay, Bronx"), [Schuylerville](/wiki/Schuylerville,_Bronx "Schuylerville, Bronx"), and [Throggs Neck](/wiki/Throgs_Neck,_Bronx "Throgs Neck, Bronx"), as well as a portion of Queens.
* [Ritchie Torres](/wiki/Ritchie_Torres "Ritchie Torres") (first elected in 2020) represents [New York's 15th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_15th_congressional_district "New York's 15th congressional district"), which includes [West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx") and [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx").
* [George Latimer](/wiki/George_Latimer_(New_York_politician) "George Latimer (New York politician)") (first elected in 2024) represents [New York's 16th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_16th_congressional_district "New York's 16th congressional district"), which includes the neighborhood of [Wakefield](/wiki/Wakefield,_Bronx "Wakefield, Bronx"), as well as the southern half of Westchester County.

### Elections for Mayor of New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Elections for Mayor of New York")]

Main article: [New York City mayoral elections](/wiki/New_York_City_mayoral_elections "New York City mayoral elections")

The Bronx has often shown striking differences from other boroughs in [elections for Mayor](/wiki/New_York_City_mayoral_elections "New York City mayoral elections"). The only Republican to carry the Bronx since 1914 was [Fiorello La Guardia](/wiki/Fiorello_La_Guardia "Fiorello La Guardia") in 1933, 1937, and 1941 (and in the latter two elections, only because his 30% to 32% vote on the [American Labor Party](/wiki/American_Labor_Party "American Labor Party") line was added to 22% to 23% as a Republican).[[212]](#cite_note-212) The Bronx was thus the only borough not carried by the successful Republican re-election campaigns of Mayors [Rudy Giuliani](/wiki/Rudy_Giuliani "Rudy Giuliani") in 1997 and [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") in 2005. The anti-war [Socialist](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") campaign of [Morris Hillquit](/wiki/Morris_Hillquit "Morris Hillquit") in the [1917 mayoral election](/wiki/1917_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1917 New York City mayoral election") won over 31% of the Bronx's vote, putting him second and well ahead of the 20% won by the incumbent pro-war Fusion Mayor [John Purroy Mitchel](/wiki/John_Purroy_Mitchel "John Purroy Mitchel"), who came in second (ahead of Hillquit) everywhere else and outpolled Hillquit citywide by 23.2% to 21.7%.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [Education in New York City](/wiki/Education_in_New_York_City "Education in New York City"), [List of public elementary schools in New York City](/wiki/List_of_public_elementary_schools_in_New_York_City "List of public elementary schools in New York City"), and [Category:Charter schools in New York (state)](/wiki/Category:Charter_schools_in_New_York_(state) "Category:Charter schools in New York (state)")

Education in the Bronx is provided by a large number of public and private institutions, many of which draw students who live beyond the Bronx. The [New York City Department of Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Education "New York City Department of Education") manages the borough's public noncharter schools.[[213]](#cite_note-213) In 2000, public schools enrolled nearly 280,000 of the Bronx's residents over three years old (out of 333,100 enrolled in all pre-college schools).[[214]](#cite_note-quicktable-214)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] There are also several public [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools "Charter schools"). Private schools range from elite [independent schools](/wiki/Independent_school "Independent school") to religiously affiliated [schools](/wiki/Parochial_schools#United_States "Parochial schools") run by the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_New_York "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York") and Jewish organizations.

A small portion of land between Pelham and Pelham Bay Park, with 35 houses, is a part of the Bronx, but is cut off from the rest of the borough due to the county boundaries; the New York City government pays for the residents' children to go to [Pelham Union Free School District](/wiki/Pelham_Union_Free_School_District "Pelham Union Free School District") schools, including [Pelham Memorial High School](/wiki/Pelham_Memorial_High_School "Pelham Memorial High School"), since that is more cost effective than sending school buses to take the students to New York City schools. This arrangement has been in place since 1948.[[215]](#cite_note-gross19970506-215)

### Educational attainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Educational attainment")]

In 2000, according to the United States census, out of the nearly 800,000 people in the Bronx who were then at least 25 years old, 62.3% had graduated from high school and 14.6% held a bachelor's or higher college degree. These percentages were lower than those for New York's other boroughs, which ranged from 68.8% (Brooklyn) to 82.6% (Staten Island) for high school graduates over 24, and from 21.8% (Brooklyn) to 49.4% (Manhattan) for college graduates. (The respective state and national percentages were *[NY]* 79.1% & 27.4% and *[US]* 80.4% & 24.4%.)[[216]](#cite_note-216)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

### High schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: High schools")]

See also: [List of high schools in New York City § Bronx](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_New_York_City#Bronx "List of high schools in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/BronxScience.jpg/250px-BronxScience.jpg)](/wiki/File:BronxScience.jpg)

[The Bronx High School of Science](/wiki/The_Bronx_High_School_of_Science "The Bronx High School of Science")

In the 2000 Census, 79,240 of the nearly 95,000 Bronx residents enrolled in high school attended public schools.[[214]](#cite_note-quicktable-214)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Many public [high schools](/wiki/High_school_(North_America) "High school (North America)") are in the borough including the elite [Bronx High School of Science](/wiki/Bronx_High_School_of_Science "Bronx High School of Science"), [Celia Cruz Bronx High School of Music](/wiki/Celia_Cruz_Bronx_High_School_of_Music "Celia Cruz Bronx High School of Music"), [DeWitt Clinton High School](/wiki/DeWitt_Clinton_High_School "DeWitt Clinton High School"), [High School for Violin and Dance](/wiki/High_School_for_Violin_and_Dance "High School for Violin and Dance"), [Bronx Leadership Academy 2](/wiki/Bronx_Leadership_Academy_2 "Bronx Leadership Academy 2"), [Bronx International High School](/wiki/Bronx_International_High_School "Bronx International High School"), the [School for Excellence](/wiki/School_for_Excellence "School for Excellence"), the [Morris Academy for Collaborative Study](/wiki/Morris_Academy_for_Collaborative_Study "Morris Academy for Collaborative Study"), Wings Academy for young adults, The [Bronx School for Law, Government and Justice](/wiki/Bronx_School_for_Law,_Government_and_Justice "Bronx School for Law, Government and Justice"), [Validus Preparatory Academy](/wiki/Validus_Preparatory_Academy "Validus Preparatory Academy"), The Eagle Academy For Young Men, Bronx Expeditionary Learning High School, Bronx Academy of Letters, [Herbert H. Lehman High School](/wiki/Herbert_H._Lehman_High_School "Herbert H. Lehman High School") and [High School of American Studies](/wiki/High_School_of_American_Studies_at_Lehman_College "High School of American Studies at Lehman College"). The Bronx is also home to three of New York City's most prestigious private, secular schools: [Fieldston](/wiki/Ethical_Culture_Fieldston_School "Ethical Culture Fieldston School"), [Horace Mann](/wiki/Horace_Mann_School "Horace Mann School"), and [Riverdale Country School](/wiki/Riverdale_Country_School "Riverdale Country School").

High schools linked to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") include: [St. Raymond Academy for Girls](/wiki/St._Raymond_Academy_for_Girls "St. Raymond Academy for Girls"), [All Hallows High School](/wiki/All_Hallows_High_School "All Hallows High School"), [Fordham Preparatory School](/wiki/Fordham_Preparatory_School "Fordham Preparatory School"), [Monsignor Scanlan High School](/wiki/Monsignor_Scanlan_High_School "Monsignor Scanlan High School"), [St. Raymond High School for Boys](/wiki/St._Raymond_High_School_for_Boys "St. Raymond High School for Boys"), [Cardinal Hayes High School](/wiki/Cardinal_Hayes_High_School "Cardinal Hayes High School"), [Cardinal Spellman High School](/wiki/Cardinal_Spellman_High_School_(New_York_City) "Cardinal Spellman High School (New York City)"), [The Academy of Mount Saint Ursula](/wiki/Academy_of_Mount_St._Ursula_High_School "Academy of Mount St. Ursula High School"), [Aquinas High School](/wiki/Aquinas_High_School_(New_York_City) "Aquinas High School (New York City)"), [Preston High School](/wiki/Preston_High_School_(New_York_City) "Preston High School (New York City)"), [St. Catharine Academy](/wiki/St._Catharine_Academy "St. Catharine Academy"), [Mount Saint Michael Academy](/wiki/Mount_Saint_Michael_Academy "Mount Saint Michael Academy"), and [St. Barnabas High School](/wiki/St._Barnabas_High_School "St. Barnabas High School").

The [SAR Academy](/wiki/SAR_Academy "SAR Academy") and [SAR High School](/wiki/SAR_High_School "SAR High School") are [Modern Orthodox](/wiki/Modern_Orthodox "Modern Orthodox") Jewish [Yeshiva](/wiki/Yeshiva "Yeshiva") coeducational day schools in [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx"), with roots in Manhattan's [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side").

In the 1990s, New York City began closing the large, public high schools in the Bronx and replacing them with small high schools. Among the reasons cited for the changes were poor graduation rates and concerns about safety. Schools that have been closed or reduced in size include [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_High_School_(Bronx,_New_York) "John F. Kennedy High School (Bronx, New York)"), [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe_High_School_(New_York) "James Monroe High School (New York)"), [Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft_High_School_(New_York_City) "William Howard Taft High School (New York City)"), [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_High_School_(New_York_City) "Theodore Roosevelt High School (New York City)"), [Adlai Stevenson](/wiki/Adlai_E._Stevenson_High_School_(New_York_City) "Adlai E. Stevenson High School (New York City)"), [Evander Childs](/wiki/Evander_Childs_High_School "Evander Childs High School"), [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus_High_School_(Bronx,_New_York) "Christopher Columbus High School (Bronx, New York)"), [Morris](/wiki/Morris_High_School_(Bronx,_New_York) "Morris High School (Bronx, New York)"), [Walton](/wiki/Walton_High_School_(New_York_City) "Walton High School (New York City)"), and South Bronx High Schools.

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

See also: [List of colleges and universities in New York City](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_New_York_City "List of colleges and universities in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Fordham_University_Keating_Hall.JPG/250px-Fordham_University_Keating_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fordham_University_Keating_Hall.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/AECOM.jpg/330px-AECOM.jpg)](/wiki/File:AECOM.jpg)

The Bronx hosts [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University") and the [Albert Einstein College of Medicine](/wiki/Albert_Einstein_College_of_Medicine "Albert Einstein College of Medicine"), among others.

In 2000, 49,442 (57.5%) of the 86,014 Bronx residents seeking college, graduate or professional degrees attended public institutions.[[214]](#cite_note-quicktable-214) Several colleges and universities are in the Bronx.

[Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University") was founded as St. John's College in 1841 by the [Diocese of New York](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_New_York "Archdiocese of New York") as the first Catholic institution of higher education in the [northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"). It is now officially an independent institution, but strongly embraces its [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") heritage. The 85-acre (340,000 m2) Bronx campus, known as Rose Hill, is the main campus of the university, and is among the largest within the city (other Fordham campuses are in Manhattan and Westchester County).[[116]](#cite_note-fordzoo-116)

Three campuses of the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") are in the Bronx: [Hostos Community College](/wiki/Hostos_Community_College "Hostos Community College"), [Bronx Community College](/wiki/Bronx_Community_College "Bronx Community College") (occupying the former [University Heights](/wiki/University_Heights,_Bronx "University Heights, Bronx") Campus of [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"))[[217]](#cite_note-217) and Herbert H. [Lehman College](/wiki/Lehman_College "Lehman College") (formerly the uptown campus of [Hunter College](/wiki/Hunter_College "Hunter College")), which offers both undergraduate and graduate degrees.

The [College of Mount Saint Vincent](/wiki/College_of_Mount_Saint_Vincent "College of Mount Saint Vincent") is a Catholic liberal arts college in [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx") under the direction of the [Sisters of Charity of New York](/wiki/Sisters_of_Charity_of_New_York "Sisters of Charity of New York"). Founded in 1847 as a school for girls, the academy became a degree-granting college in 1911 and began admitting men in 1974. The school serves 1,600 students. Its campus is also home to the [Academy for Jewish Religion](/wiki/Academy_for_Jewish_Religion_(New_York) "Academy for Jewish Religion (New York)"), a transdenominational rabbinical and cantorial school.

[Manhattan University](/wiki/Manhattan_University "Manhattan University") is a Catholic college in [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx") which offers undergraduate programs in the arts, business, education, engineering, and science. It also offers graduate programs in education and engineering.

[Albert Einstein College of Medicine](/wiki/Albert_Einstein_College_of_Medicine "Albert Einstein College of Medicine"), part of the [Montefiore Medical Center](/wiki/Montefiore_Medical_Center "Montefiore Medical Center"), is in [Morris Park](/wiki/Morris_Park,_Bronx "Morris Park, Bronx").

The coeducational and non-sectarian [Mercy University](/wiki/Mercy_University "Mercy University")—with its main campus in [Dobbs Ferry](/wiki/Dobbs_Ferry,_New_York "Dobbs Ferry, New York")—has a Bronx campus near [Westchester Square](/wiki/Westchester_Square,_Bronx "Westchester Square, Bronx").

The [State University of New York Maritime College](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_Maritime_College "State University of New York Maritime College") in [Fort Schuyler](/wiki/Fort_Schuyler "Fort Schuyler") ([Throggs Neck](/wiki/Throggs_Neck "Throggs Neck"))—at the far southeastern tip of the Bronx—is the national leader in maritime education and houses the [Maritime Industry Museum](/wiki/Maritime_Industry_Museum "Maritime Industry Museum"). (Directly across [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") is [Kings Point](/wiki/Kings_Point,_New_York "Kings Point, New York"), Long Island, home of the [United States Merchant Marine Academy](/wiki/United_States_Merchant_Marine_Academy "United States Merchant Marine Academy") and the American Merchant Marine Museum.) As of 2017, graduates from the university earned an average annual salary of $144,000, the highest of any university graduates in the United States.[[218]](#cite_note-218)

In addition, the private, proprietary [Monroe University](/wiki/Monroe_University "Monroe University"), focused on preparation for business and the professions, started in the Bronx in 1933 and now has a campus in [New Rochelle](/wiki/New_Rochelle,_New_York "New Rochelle, New York") (Westchester County) as well the Bronx's [Fordham](/wiki/Fordham,_Bronx "Fordham, Bronx") neighborhood.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Transportation")]

See also: [Transportation in New York City](/wiki/Transportation_in_New_York_City "Transportation in New York City")

### Roads and streets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Roads and streets")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Bronx-Whitestone_Bridge.jpg/330px-Bronx-Whitestone_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronx-Whitestone_Bridge.jpg)

[Bronx–Whitestone Bridge](/wiki/Bronx%E2%80%93Whitestone_Bridge "Bronx–Whitestone Bridge")

#### Surface streets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Surface streets")]

The Bronx [street grid](/wiki/Street_grid "Street grid") is irregular. Like the northernmost part of [upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan"), the [West Bronx](/wiki/West_Bronx "West Bronx")'s hilly terrain leaves a relatively free-style street grid. Much of the West Bronx's street numbering carries over from upper Manhattan, but does not match it exactly; East 132nd Street is the lowest numbered street in the Bronx. This dates from the mid-19th century when the southwestern area of [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") west of the Bronx River, was incorporated into New York City and known as the Northside.

The [East Bronx](/wiki/East_Bronx "East Bronx") is considerably flatter, and the street layout tends to be more regular. Only the [Wakefield](/wiki/Wakefield,_Bronx "Wakefield, Bronx") neighborhood picks up the street numbering, albeit at a misalignment due to Tremont Avenue's layout. At the same diagonal latitude, West 262nd Street in Riverdale matches East 237th Street in Wakefield.

Three major north–south thoroughfares run between Manhattan and the Bronx: [Third Avenue](/wiki/Third_Avenue "Third Avenue"), [Park Avenue](/wiki/Park_Avenue "Park Avenue"), and [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)"). Other major north–south roads include the [Grand Concourse](/wiki/Grand_Concourse_(Bronx) "Grand Concourse (Bronx)"), [Jerome Avenue](/wiki/Jerome_Avenue "Jerome Avenue"), [Sedgwick Avenue](/wiki/Sedgwick_Avenue "Sedgwick Avenue"), [Webster Avenue](/wiki/Webster_Avenue "Webster Avenue"), and [White Plains Road](/wiki/White_Plains_Road "White Plains Road"). Major east-west thoroughfares include [Mosholu Parkway](/wiki/Mosholu_Parkway "Mosholu Parkway"), [Gun Hill Road](/wiki/Gun_Hill_Road_(Bronx) "Gun Hill Road (Bronx)"), [Fordham Road](/wiki/Fordham_Road "Fordham Road"), [Pelham Parkway](/wiki/Pelham_Parkway "Pelham Parkway"), and Tremont Avenue.

Most east–west streets are prefixed with either *East* or *West*, to indicate on which side of Jerome Avenue they lie (continuing the similar system in Manhattan, which uses [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") as the dividing line).[[220]](#cite_note-220)

The historic [Boston Post Road](/wiki/Boston_Post_Road "Boston Post Road"), part of the long pre-revolutionary road connecting [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") with other northeastern cities, runs east–west in some places, and sometimes northeast–southwest.

[Mosholu](/wiki/Mosholu_Parkway "Mosholu Parkway") and [Pelham Parkways](/wiki/Pelham_Parkway "Pelham Parkway"), with [Bronx Park](/wiki/Bronx_Park "Bronx Park") between them, [Van Cortlandt Park](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park") to the west and [Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park") to the east, are also linked by [bridle paths](/wiki/Bridle_path "Bridle path").

As of the 2000 Census, approximately 61.6% of all Bronx households do not have access to a car. Citywide, the percentage of autoless households is 55%.[[221]](#cite_note-221)

#### Highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Highways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/2024-05-21_14_29_44_View_north_along_Interstate_95_and_U.S._Route_1_and_east_along_the_Cross_Bronx_Expressway_from_the_overpass_for_Macombs_Road_in_the_Bronx%2C_New_York_City%2C_New_York.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:2024-05-21_14_29_44_View_north_along_Interstate_95_and_U.S._Route_1_and_east_along_the_Cross_Bronx_Expressway_from_the_overpass_for_Macombs_Road_in_the_Bronx,_New_York_City,_New_York.jpg)

The Cross Bronx Expressway (I-95/US 1) is typically clogged with traffic

Several major [limited access](/wiki/Limited_access "Limited access") highways traverse the Bronx. These include:

* the [Bronx River Parkway](/wiki/Bronx_River_Parkway "Bronx River Parkway")
* the [Bruckner Expressway](/wiki/Bruckner_Expressway "Bruckner Expressway") ([I-278](/wiki/Interstate_278 "Interstate 278")/[I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_New_York "Interstate 95 in New York"))
* the [Cross Bronx Expressway](/wiki/Cross_Bronx_Expressway "Cross Bronx Expressway") ([I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_New_York "Interstate 95 in New York")/[I-295](/wiki/Interstate_295_(New_York) "Interstate 295 (New York)"))
* the [New England Thruway](/wiki/New_England_Thruway "New England Thruway") ([I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_New_York "Interstate 95 in New York"))
* the [Henry Hudson Parkway](/wiki/Henry_Hudson_Parkway "Henry Hudson Parkway") ([NY-9A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_9A "New York State Route 9A"))
* the [Hutchinson River Parkway](/wiki/Hutchinson_River_Parkway "Hutchinson River Parkway")
* the [Major Deegan Expressway](/wiki/Major_Deegan_Expressway "Major Deegan Expressway") ([I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)"))

#### Bridges and tunnels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Bridges and tunnels")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Aerial_View_of_the_Throgs_Neck_Bridge.jpg/330px-Aerial_View_of_the_Throgs_Neck_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_View_of_the_Throgs_Neck_Bridge.jpg)

An aerial view of the [Throgs Neck Bridge](/wiki/Throgs_Neck_Bridge "Throgs Neck Bridge")

Thirteen bridges and three tunnels connect the Bronx to Manhattan, and three bridges connect the Bronx to [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"). These are, from west to east:

*To Manhattan:* the [Spuyten Duyvil Bridge](/wiki/Spuyten_Duyvil_Bridge "Spuyten Duyvil Bridge"), the [Henry Hudson Bridge](/wiki/Henry_Hudson_Bridge "Henry Hudson Bridge"), the [Broadway Bridge](/wiki/Broadway_Bridge_(Manhattan) "Broadway Bridge (Manhattan)"), the [University Heights Bridge](/wiki/University_Heights_Bridge "University Heights Bridge"), the [Washington Bridge](/wiki/Washington_Bridge_(Harlem_River) "Washington Bridge (Harlem River)"), the [Alexander Hamilton Bridge](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton_Bridge "Alexander Hamilton Bridge"), the [High Bridge](/wiki/High_Bridge_(New_York_City) "High Bridge (New York City)"), the [Concourse Tunnel](/wiki/Concourse_Tunnel "Concourse Tunnel"), the [Macombs Dam Bridge](/wiki/Macombs_Dam_Bridge "Macombs Dam Bridge"), the [145th Street Bridge](/wiki/145th_Street_Bridge "145th Street Bridge"), the [149th Street Tunnel](/wiki/149th_Street_Tunnel "149th Street Tunnel"), the [Madison Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Madison_Avenue_Bridge "Madison Avenue Bridge"), the [Park Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Park_Avenue_Bridge_(New_York_City) "Park Avenue Bridge (New York City)"), the [Lexington Avenue Tunnel](/wiki/Lexington_Avenue_Tunnel "Lexington Avenue Tunnel"), the [Third Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Third_Avenue_Bridge_(Manhattan) "Third Avenue Bridge (Manhattan)") (southbound traffic only), and the [Willis Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Willis_Avenue_Bridge "Willis Avenue Bridge") (northbound traffic only).

*To both Manhattan and Queens:* the [Robert F. Kennedy Bridge](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Bridge "Robert F. Kennedy Bridge"), formerly known as the Triborough Bridge.

*To Queens:* the [Bronx–Whitestone Bridge](/wiki/Bronx%E2%80%93Whitestone_Bridge "Bronx–Whitestone Bridge") and the [Throgs Neck Bridge](/wiki/Throgs_Neck_Bridge "Throgs Neck Bridge").

### Mass transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Mass transit")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Middletown_Road_%28IRT_Pelham_Line%29_by_David_Shankbone_copy.jpg/330px-Middletown_Road_%28IRT_Pelham_Line%29_by_David_Shankbone_copy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Middletown_Road_(IRT_Pelham_Line)_by_David_Shankbone_copy.jpg)

[Middletown Road](/wiki/Middletown_Road_(IRT_Pelham_Line) "Middletown Road (IRT Pelham Line)") subway station on the [6](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)") and [<6>](/wiki/6d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6d (New York City Subway service)")​ trains

The Bronx is served by seven [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") services along six physical lines, with [70 stations in the Bronx](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_stations_in_the_Bronx "List of New York City Subway stations in the Bronx"):[[222]](#cite_note-222)

* [IND Concourse Line](/wiki/IND_Concourse_Line "IND Concourse Line") ([B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)") and ​[D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)") trains)
* [IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line "IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line") ([1](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)") train)
* [IRT Dyre Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Dyre_Avenue_Line "IRT Dyre Avenue Line") ([5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)") train)
* [IRT Jerome Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Jerome_Avenue_Line "IRT Jerome Avenue Line") ([4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)") train)
* [IRT Pelham Line](/wiki/IRT_Pelham_Line "IRT Pelham Line") ([6](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)") and [<6>](/wiki/6d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6d (New York City Subway service)")​ trains)
* [IRT White Plains Road Line](/wiki/IRT_White_Plains_Road_Line "IRT White Plains Road Line") ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)") and ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)") trains)

There are also many [MTA Regional Bus Operations](/wiki/MTA_Regional_Bus_Operations "MTA Regional Bus Operations") bus routes in the Bronx. This includes [local and express routes](/wiki/List_of_bus_routes_in_the_Bronx "List of bus routes in the Bronx") as well as [Bee-Line Bus System](/wiki/Bee-Line_Bus_System "Bee-Line Bus System") routes.[[223]](#cite_note-223)

Two [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad") commuter rail lines (the [Harlem Line](/wiki/Harlem_Line "Harlem Line") and the [Hudson Line](/wiki/Hudson_Line_(Metro-North) "Hudson Line (Metro-North)")) serve 11 stations in the Bronx. ([Marble Hill](/wiki/Marble_Hill_(Metro-North_station) "Marble Hill (Metro-North station)"), between the [Spuyten Duyvil](/wiki/Spuyten_Duyvil_(Metro-North_station) "Spuyten Duyvil (Metro-North station)") and [University Heights](/wiki/University_Heights_(Metro-North_station) "University Heights (Metro-North station)") stations, is actually in the only part of Manhattan connected to the mainland.) In addition, some trains serving the [New Haven Line](/wiki/New_Haven_Line "New Haven Line") stop at [Fordham Plaza](/wiki/Fordham_(Metro-North_station) "Fordham (Metro-North station)"). As part of [Penn Station Access](/wiki/Penn_Station_Access "Penn Station Access"), the 2018 MTA budget funded construction of four new stops along the New Haven Line to serve [Hunts Point](/wiki/Hunts_Point,_Bronx "Hunts Point, Bronx"), [Parkchester](/wiki/Parkchester,_Bronx "Parkchester, Bronx"), [Morris Park](/wiki/Morris_Park,_Bronx "Morris Park, Bronx"), and [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City,_Bronx "Co-op City, Bronx").[[224]](#cite_note-224)

In 2018, [NYC Ferry](/wiki/NYC_Ferry "NYC Ferry")'s Soundview line opened, connecting the [Soundview](/wiki/Soundview,_Bronx "Soundview, Bronx") landing in [Clason Point Park](/wiki/Clason_Point "Clason Point") to three [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") locations in Manhattan. On December 28, 2021; the Throgs Neck Ferry landing at Ferry Point Park in [Throgs Neck](/wiki/Throgs_Neck,_Bronx "Throgs Neck, Bronx") was opened providing an additional stop on the Soundview line.[[225]](#cite_note-225) The ferry is operated by [Hornblower Cruises](/wiki/Hornblower_Cruises "Hornblower Cruises").[[226]](#cite_note-226)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

### Film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Film and television")]

See also: [List of films set in New York City](/wiki/List_of_films_set_in_New_York_City "List of films set in New York City") and [List of television shows set in New York City](/wiki/List_of_television_shows_set_in_New_York_City "List of television shows set in New York City")

#### Mid-20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Mid-20th century")]

Mid-20th century movies set in the Bronx portrayed densely settled, working-class, urban culture. *[From This Day Forward](/wiki/From_This_Day_Forward "From This Day Forward")* (1946), set in [Highbridge](/wiki/Highbridge,_Bronx "Highbridge, Bronx"), occasionally delved into Bronx life. The most notable examinations of working class Bronx life were [Paddy Chayefsky](/wiki/Paddy_Chayefsky "Paddy Chayefsky")'s [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")-winning *[Marty](/wiki/Marty_(film) "Marty (film)")*[[228]](#cite_note-228) and his 1956 film *[The Catered Affair](/wiki/The_Catered_Affair "The Catered Affair").* Other films that portrayed life in the Bronx are: the 1993 [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro")/[Chazz Palminteri](/wiki/Chazz_Palminteri "Chazz Palminteri") film, *[A Bronx Tale](/wiki/A_Bronx_Tale "A Bronx Tale")*, [Spike Lee](/wiki/Spike_Lee "Spike Lee")'s 1999 movie *[Summer of Sam](/wiki/Summer_of_Sam "Summer of Sam")*, which focused on an [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American") Bronx community in the 1970s, 1994's *[I Like It Like That](/wiki/I_Like_It_Like_That_(film) "I Like It Like That (film)")* which takes place in the predominantly [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people") neighborhood of the South Bronx, and *Doughboys*, the story of two Italian-American brothers in danger of losing their bakery thanks to one brother's gambling debts.

The Bronx's gritty urban life had worked its way into the movies even earlier, with depictions of the "[Bronx cheer](/wiki/Bronx_cheer_(gesture) "Bronx cheer (gesture)")", a loud flatulent-like sound of disapproval, allegedly first made by [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") fans. The sound can be heard, for example, on the [Spike Jones](/wiki/Spike_Jones "Spike Jones") and His City Slickers recording of "Der Fuehrer's Face" (from the 1942 [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney") [animated film](/wiki/Animated_film "Animated film") of the [same name](/wiki/Der_Fuehrer%27s_Face "Der Fuehrer's Face")), repeatedly lambasting [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") with: "We'll Heil! (Bronx cheer) Heil! (Bronx cheer) Right in Der Fuehrer's Face!"[[229]](#cite_note-229)[[230]](#cite_note-230)

#### Symbolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Symbolism")]

Starting in the 1970s, the Bronx often symbolized violence, decay, and urban ruin. The wave of arson in the South Bronx in the 1960s and 1970s inspired the observation that "The Bronx is burning": in 1974 it was the title of both an editorial in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* and a [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") [documentary film](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film").[[231]](#cite_note-231) The line entered the pop-consciousness with Game Two of the [1977 World Series](/wiki/1977_World_Series "1977 World Series"), when a fire broke out near [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium_(1923) "Yankee Stadium (1923)") as the team was playing the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers"). As the fire was captured on live television, announcer [Howard Cosell](/wiki/Howard_Cosell "Howard Cosell") [is wrongly remembered to have said something like](/wiki/The_Bronx_Is_Burning#Summary "The Bronx Is Burning"), "There it is, ladies and gentlemen: the Bronx is burning". Historians of New York City often point to Cosell's remark as an acknowledgement of both the city and the borough's decline.[[232]](#cite_note-232) A feature-length documentary film by Edwin Pagán called *Bronx Burning* chronicled what led up to the many arson-for-insurance fraud fires of the 1970s in the borough.[[233]](#cite_note-233)[[234]](#cite_note-234)

Bronx gang life was depicted in the 1974 novel *The Wanderers* by Bronx native [Richard Price](/wiki/Richard_Price_(writer) "Richard Price (writer)") and the [1979 movie of the same name](/wiki/The_Wanderers_(1979_film) "The Wanderers (1979 film)"). They are set in the heart of the Bronx, showing apartment life and the then-landmark Krums ice cream parlor. In the 1979 film *[The Warriors](/wiki/The_Warriors_(film) "The Warriors (film)")*, the eponymous gang go to a meeting in [Van Cortlandt Park](/wiki/Van_Cortlandt_Park "Van Cortlandt Park") in the Bronx, and have to fight their way out of the borough and get back to [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). The 2005 video game adaptation features levels called Pelham, Tremont, and "Gunhill" (a play off the name [Gun Hill Road](/wiki/Gun_Hill_Road_(Bronx) "Gun Hill Road (Bronx)")). *[A Bronx Tale](/wiki/A_Bronx_Tale "A Bronx Tale")* (1993) depicts gang activities in the [Belmont](/wiki/Belmont,_Bronx "Belmont, Bronx") "Little Italy" section of the Bronx. This theme lends itself to the title of *[The Bronx Is Burning](/wiki/The_Bronx_Is_Burning "The Bronx Is Burning")*, an eight-part [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") TV mini-series (2007) about the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees")' drive to winning baseball's [1977 World Series](/wiki/1977_World_Series "1977 World Series"). The TV series emphasizes the team's boisterous nature, led by manager [Billy Martin](/wiki/Billy_Martin "Billy Martin"), catcher [Thurman Munson](/wiki/Thurman_Munson "Thurman Munson") and outfielder [Reggie Jackson](/wiki/Reggie_Jackson "Reggie Jackson"), as well as the malaise of the Bronx and New York City in general during that time, such as the blackout, the city's serious financial woes and near bankruptcy, the arson for insurance payments, and the election of [Ed Koch](/wiki/Ed_Koch "Ed Koch") as mayor.

The 1981 film *[Fort Apache, The Bronx](/wiki/Fort_Apache,_The_Bronx "Fort Apache, The Bronx")* is another film that used the Bronx's gritty image for its storyline. The movie's title is from the nickname for the 41st Police Precinct in the South Bronx which was nicknamed "Fort Apache". Also from 1981 is the horror film *[Wolfen](/wiki/Wolfen_(film) "Wolfen (film)")* making use of the rubble of the Bronx as a home for werewolf type creatures. *[Knights of the South Bronx](/wiki/Knights_of_the_South_Bronx "Knights of the South Bronx")*, a true story of a teacher who worked with disadvantaged children, is another film also set in the Bronx released in 2005. The Bronx was the setting for the 1983 film *[Fuga dal Bronx](/wiki/Fuga_dal_Bronx "Fuga dal Bronx")*, also known as *Bronx Warriors 2* and *Escape 2000*, an Italian B-movie best known for its appearance on the television series *[Mystery Science Theater 3000](/wiki/Mystery_Science_Theater_3000 "Mystery Science Theater 3000")*. The plot revolves around a sinister construction corporation's plans to depopulate, destroy and redevelop the Bronx, and a band of rebels who are out to expose the corporation's murderous ways and save their homes. The film is memorable for its almost incessant use of the phrase, "Leave the Bronx!" Many of the movie's scenes were filmed in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), substituting as the Bronx. *[Rumble in the Bronx](/wiki/Rumble_in_the_Bronx "Rumble in the Bronx")*, filmed in Vancouver, was a 1995 [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") [kung-fu](/wiki/Kung-fu "Kung-fu") film, another which popularized the Bronx to international audiences. *[Last Bronx](/wiki/Last_Bronx "Last Bronx")*, a 1996 Sega game played on the bad reputation of the Bronx to lend its name to an alternate version of post-Japanese bubble Tokyo, where crime and gang warfare is rampant. The 2016 [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") series *[The Get Down](/wiki/The_Get_Down "The Get Down")* is based on the development of hip hop in 1977 in the South Bronx.[[235]](#cite_note-235)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Literature")]

See also: [List of books set in New York City](/wiki/List_of_books_set_in_New_York_City "List of books set in New York City")

#### Books

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Books")]

The Bronx has been featured significantly in fiction literature. All of the characters in [Herman Wouk](/wiki/Herman_Wouk "Herman Wouk")'s [City Boy: The Adventures of Herbie Bookbinder](/wiki/City_Boy:_The_Adventures_of_Herbie_Bookbinder "City Boy: The Adventures of Herbie Bookbinder") (1948) live in the Bronx, and about half of the action is set there. [Kate Simon](/wiki/Kate_Simon "Kate Simon")'s *Bronx Primitive: Portraits of a Childhood* (1982) is directly autobiographical, a warm account of a Polish-Jewish girl in an immigrant family growing up before World War II, and living near [Arthur Avenue](/wiki/Arthur_Avenue "Arthur Avenue") and [Tremont Avenue](/wiki/Tremont_Avenue "Tremont Avenue").[[236]](#cite_note-236) In Jacob M. Appel's short story, "The Grand Concourse" (2007),[[237]](#cite_note-237) a woman who grew up in the iconic [Lewis Morris](/wiki/Lewis_Morris "Lewis Morris") Building returns to the [Morrisania](/wiki/Morrisania "Morrisania") neighborhood with her adult daughter. Similarly, in [Avery Corman](/wiki/Avery_Corman "Avery Corman")'s book *The Old Neighborhood* (1980),[[238]](#cite_note-238) an upper-middle class white protagonist returns to his birth neighborhood ([Fordham Road](/wiki/Fordham_Road "Fordham Road") and the [Grand Concourse](/wiki/Grand_Concourse_(Bronx) "Grand Concourse (Bronx)")), and learns that even though the folks are poor, Hispanic and African-American, they are good people.

By contrast, [Tom Wolfe](/wiki/Tom_Wolfe "Tom Wolfe")'s *[Bonfire of the Vanities](/wiki/The_Bonfire_of_the_Vanities "The Bonfire of the Vanities")* (1987)[[239]](#cite_note-239) portrays a wealthy, white protagonist, Sherman McCoy, getting lost off the [Bruckner Expressway](/wiki/Bruckner_Expressway "Bruckner Expressway") in the [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx") and having an altercation with locals. A substantial piece of the last part of the book is set in the resulting riotous trial at the Bronx County Courthouse. However, times change, and in 2007, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that "the Bronx neighborhoods near the site of Sherman's accident are now dotted with townhouses and apartments." In the same article, the Reverend [Al Sharpton](/wiki/Al_Sharpton "Al Sharpton") (whose fictional analogue in the novel is "Reverend Bacon") asserts that "twenty years later, the cynicism of *The Bonfire of the Vanities* is as out of style as [Tom Wolfe](/wiki/Tom_Wolfe "Tom Wolfe")'s wardrobe."[[240]](#cite_note-240)

[Don DeLillo](/wiki/Don_DeLillo "Don DeLillo")'s *[Underworld](/wiki/Underworld_(DeLillo_novel) "Underworld (DeLillo novel)")* (1997) is also set in the Bronx and offers a perspective on the area from the 1950s onward.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

#### Poetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Poetry")]

In poetry, the Bronx has been immortalized by one of the world's shortest [couplets](/wiki/Couplet "Couplet"):

The Bronx?  
No Thonx  
 [Ogden Nash](/wiki/Ogden_Nash "Ogden Nash"), *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*, 1931

Nash repented 33 years after his [calumny](/wiki/Calumny "Calumny"), penning the following poem to the dean of faculty at [Bronx Community College](/wiki/Bronx_Community_College "Bronx Community College") in 1964:[[242]](#cite_note-242)

I wrote those lines, "The Bronx? No thonx";  
I shudder to confess them.  
  
Now I'm an older, wiser man  
I cry, "The Bronx? God bless them!"[[89]](#cite_note-thonx-89)

In 2016, W. R. Rodriguez published *Bronx Trilogy*—consisting of *the shoe shine parlor poems et al.*, *concrete pastures of the beautiful bronx*, and *from the banks of brook avenue*. The trilogy celebrates Bronx people, places, and events. [DeWitt Clinton High School](/wiki/DeWitt_Clinton_High_School "DeWitt Clinton High School"), [St. Mary's Park](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Park_(Bronx) "St. Mary's Park (Bronx)"), and Brook Avenue are a few of the schools, parks, and streets Rodriguez uses as subjects for his poems.[[243]](#cite_note-243)

Nash's couplet "The Bronx? No Thonx" and his subsequent blessing are mentioned in *Bronx Accent: A Literary and Pictorial History of the Borough*, edited by Lloyd Ultan and Barbara Unger and published in 2000. The book, which includes the work of Yiddish poets, offers a selection from [Allen Ginsberg](/wiki/Allen_Ginsberg "Allen Ginsberg")'s *[Kaddish](/wiki/Kaddish_(poem) "Kaddish (poem)")*, as his Aunt Elanor and his mother, Naomi, lived near Woodlawn Cemetery. Also featured is Ruth Lisa Schecther's poem, "Bronx", which is described as a celebration of the borough's landmarks. There is a selection of works from poets such as [Sandra María Esteves](/wiki/Sandra_Mar%C3%ADa_Esteves "Sandra María Esteves"), [Milton Kessler](/wiki/Milton_Kessler "Milton Kessler"), Joan Murray, W. R. Rodriguez, Myra Shapiro, Gayl Teller, and [Terence Wynch](/wiki/Terence_Winch "Terence Winch").[[244]](#cite_note-244)

"Bronx Migrations" by Michelle M. Tokarczyk is a collection that spans five decades of Tokarczyk's life in the Bronx, from her exodus in 1962 to her return in search of her childhood tenement.[[245]](#cite_note-245)[[246]](#cite_note-246)

#### Bronx Memoir Project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Bronx Memoir Project")]

*[Bronx Memoir Project: Vol. 1](/wiki/Bronx_Memoir_Project:_Vol._1 "Bronx Memoir Project: Vol. 1")* is a published [anthology](/wiki/Anthology "Anthology") by the Bronx Council on the Arts and brought forth through a series of workshops meant to empower Bronx residents and shed the stigma on the Bronx's burning past.[[247]](#cite_note-NYDNI-247) The Bronx Memoir Project was created as an ongoing collaboration between the [Bronx Council on the Arts](/wiki/Bronx_Council_on_the_Arts "Bronx Council on the Arts") and other [cultural institutions](/wiki/Cultural_institution "Cultural institution"), including the Bronx [Documentary](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") Center, the [Bronx Library Center](/wiki/Bronx_Library_Center "Bronx Library Center"), the (Edgar Allan) [Poe Park Visitor Center](/wiki/Poe_Park_Visitor_Center "Poe Park Visitor Center"), Mindbuilders, and other institutions and funded through a grant from the [National Endowment for the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts").[[248]](#cite_note-HP-248)[[249]](#cite_note-AT-249) The goal was to develop and refine memoir fragments written by people of all walks of life that share a common bond residing within the Bronx.[[248]](#cite_note-HP-248)

### Songs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Songs")]

See also: [List of songs about New York City](/wiki/List_of_songs_about_New_York_City "List of songs about New York City")

* "[Jenny from the Block](/wiki/Jenny_from_the_Block "Jenny from the Block")" (2002) by [Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez "Jennifer Lopez"),[[250]](#cite_note-250)[[251]](#cite_note-251) from the album *[This is me...Then](/wiki/This_Is_Me..._Then "This Is Me... Then")* is about the South Bronx, where Lopez grew up.[[252]](#cite_note-252)
* In Marc Ferris's 5-page, 15-column list of "Songs and Compositions Inspired by New York City" in *[The Encyclopedia of New York City](/wiki/The_Encyclopedia_of_New_York_City "The Encyclopedia of New York City")* (1995),[[253]](#cite_note-253) only a handful refer to the Bronx; most refer to New York City proper, especially Manhattan and Brooklyn. Ferris's extensive but selective 1995 list mentions only four songs referring specifically to the Bronx: "On the Banks of the Bronx" (1919), by [William LeBaron](/wiki/William_LeBaron "William LeBaron") & [Victor Jacobi](/wiki/Victor_Jacobi "Victor Jacobi"); "Bronx Express" (1922), by [Henry Creamer](/wiki/Henry_Creamer "Henry Creamer"), Ossip Dymow & [Turner Layton](/wiki/Turner_Layton "Turner Layton"); "The [Tremont Avenue](/wiki/Tremont_Avenue "Tremont Avenue") Cruisewear Fashion Show" (1973), by [Jerry Livingston](/wiki/Jerry_Livingston "Jerry Livingston") & Mark David; and "I Love the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees")" (1987), by Paula Lindstrom.

### Theater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Bronx&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Theater")]

[Clifford Odets](/wiki/Clifford_Odets "Clifford Odets")'s play *[Awake and Sing!](/wiki/Awake_and_Sing! "Awake and Sing!")* is set in 1933 in the Bronx. The play, first produced at the [Belasco Theater](/wiki/Belasco_Theatre_(Broadway) "Belasco Theatre (Broadway)") in 1935, concerns a poor family living in small quarters, the struggles of the controlling parents and the aspirations of their children.[[254]](#cite_note-254)

[René Marqués](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Marqu%C3%A9s "René Marqués")' *[The Oxcart](/wiki/La_Carreta "La Carreta")* (1959) concerns a rural Puerto Rican family who immigrate to the Bronx for a better life.[[255]](#cite_note-255)

*[A Bronx Tale](/wiki/A_Bronx_Tale_(play) "A Bronx Tale (play)")* is an autobiographical [one-man show](/wiki/One-man_show "One-man show") written and performed by [Chazz Palminteri](/wiki/Chazz_Palminteri "Chazz Palminteri"). It is a coming-of-age story set in the Bronx. It premiered in Los Angeles in the 1980s and then played on Off-Broadway. After a film version involving Palminteri and Robert De Niro, Palminteri performed his one-man show on Broadway and on tour in 2007.[[256]](#cite_note-256)
